title: Jack Test
usedFor:
• Assessing hindfoot and subtalar flexibility in cases of flexible flatfoot.
  • Evaluating the mechanical action of the plantar fascia (windlass mechanism).
howTo:
• Position: Have the patient stand or sit with weight on the foot.
  • Action: Manually dorsiflex the big toe.
  • Observation: Watch for the formation of a medial arch. Dorsiflexing the toe should pull the plantar fascia, shorten the foot, and elevate the arch (windlass effect).
result:
• Positive Result (Normal/Flexible): The medial arch appears or increases when the toe is dorsiflexed.
  • Negative Result (Rigid): The arch does not change, suggesting a rigid flatfoot.
references:
• Jack Test Foot Flexibility - Orthofixar
  • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Ankle Range of Motion
usedFor: • Measuring the degrees of movement available at the ankle joint.
howTo:
• Dorsiflexion: Move the foot upwards towards the shin (Standard: 20-30°).
  • Plantar flexion: Point the foot downwards (Standard: 40-50°).
  • Pronation/Supination: Assess the rolling motion of the foot (Pronation: ~30°, Supination: ~60°).
  • Rotation: Assess slight rotational movements (Standard: ~5°).
extra:
MovementDegrees
Dorsiflexion20-30
Plantar flexion40-50
Rotation5
Pronation30
Supination60
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Ankle Anterior Drawer Test
usedFor:
• Diagnosing injuries to the Anterior Talofibular Ligament (ATFL).
  • Assessing ankle instability.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies on their back (supine) with the foot relaxed.
  • Stabilization: Hold the lower leg (tibia and fibula) steady with one hand.
  • Action: Place the foot in slight plantar flexion (20°), grasp the heel (calcaneus), and pull the foot forward.
result:
• Positive Test: Excessive forward movement (translation) of the foot compared to the healthy side.
  • Dimple Sign: A dimple may appear over the ligament area if pain is minimal.
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.
  • Miller, M. D. (2020). Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (8th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Deltoid Ligament Test
usedFor: • Assessing the medial ligaments (Deltoid ligament) of the ankle.
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with the leg hanging over the table. Stabilize the lower leg with one hand.
  • Anterior Fibers: Grasp the top of the foot; combine Eversion and Plantarflexion.
  • Middle Fibers: Grasp the heel; perform pure Eversion.
  • Posterior Fibers: Grasp the heel; combine Eversion and Dorsiflexion.
result:
• Positive Test: Pain or excessive movement (hypermobility) in the ligament area.
  • Note: A "mushy" or muscle spasm end-feel may indicate injury.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Heel Thump Test
usedFor:
• Screening for tibial stress fractures.
  • Differentiating between a high ankle sprain (syndesmosis) and a lateral sprain.
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits or lies down. Stabilize the leg with one hand.
  • Action: Use your fist to firmly thump the bottom of the patient's heel.
  • Direction: Ensure the force is applied in line with the long bone of the leg (tibia).
result:
• Syndesmosis Injury: Pain felt in the ankle joint area.
  • Stress Fracture: Pain felt along the shin bone (tibia).
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Homans Sign
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies on their back with the knee straight (extended).
  • Action: Passively push the foot up (dorsiflexion).
  • Repeat: Perform the same movement with the knee bent (flexed).
result:
• Positive Test: Deep pain in the calf muscle.
  • Warning: The book notes this test is nonspecific and unreliable; positive signs can occur with other conditions.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Mortons Neuroma Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits or lies down.
  • Action: Grasp the foot around the metatarsal heads (the ball of the foot).
  • Compression: Squeeze the metatarsals together from the sides.
result: • Positive Test: Sharp pain at the site of the neuroma, often between the 3rd and 4th toes.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Ankle Posterior Drawer Test
usedFor: • Assessing the Posterior Talofibular Ligament (PTFL) and posterior instability.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine. Foot is relaxed in slight plantar flexion (20°).
  • Stabilization: Hold the lower leg (tibia/fibula) steady.
  • Action: Grasp the heel (calcaneus) and push it backward (posteriorly) and downwards.
result:
• Positive Test: Excessive backward movement of the foot.
  • Signs: A "clunk" or suction sign may be felt/seen if the ligament is ruptured.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Ramirez Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with the knee bent and foot flat.
  • Setup: Place a blood pressure cuff around the thigh.
  • Action: Inflate the cuff to 40mm Hg and hold for 2 minutes.
result: • Positive Test: Pain increases during inflation or the patient cannot tolerate the pressure for the full duration.
accuracy: • Similar to Lowenberg's sign; use with caution.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Talar Tilt Test
usedFor:
• Assessing the Calcaneofibular Ligament (CFL).
  • Checking for lateral ankle instability.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies on their side or back. Foot is relaxed (90° angle).
  • Action: Grasp the heel and tilt it sideways (inversion and eversion).
  • Comparison: Compare the amount of tilt to the uninjured side.
result: • Positive Test: Excessive inward tilting (inversion) or pain indicates a ligament tear.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Thompson’s Test
usedFor: • Identifying a complete rupture of the Achilles Tendon.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies on their stomach (prone) with feet hanging off the table.
  • Action: Squeeze the calf muscles (gastrocnemius and soleus).
  • Observation: Watch for movement of the foot.
result: • Positive Test: The foot does not point downwards (plantar flex) when the calf is squeezed.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: External Rotation Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with the knee bent at 90°.
  • Action: Hold the foot and rotate it outwards (laterally) while keeping the ankle locked in a neutral position.
result: • Positive Test: Pain is reproduced over the syndesmosis (between the tibia and fibula).
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Squeeze Test
usedFor:
• Diagnosing syndesmotic ankle sprains (High Ankle Sprain).
  • Assessing the integrity of the interosseous membrane.
howTo:
• Action: Compress (squeeze) the tibia and fibula together at the mid-calf level.
  • Observation: Check for pain located distally at the ankle joint.
result: • Positive Test: Pain occurs at the ankle (syndesmosis), not at the spot being squeezed.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Matles Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone (on stomach) with knees bent at 90°.
  • Observation: Look at the angle of the feet.
result: • Positive Test: The injured foot falls into neutral or slight dorsiflexion (flat), unlike the healthy foot which remains slightly pointed (plantar flexed).
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Elbow Valgus Stress Test
usedFor:
• Assessing the integrity of the Medial Ulnar Collateral Ligament (UCL).
  • Primary test for medial elbow instability.
howTo:
• Position: Flex the patient's elbow to 20-30° to unlock the olecranon bone from its fossa.
  • Action: Stabilize the patient's wrist with one hand and support the elbow with the other.
  • Force: Apply a valgus (inward) force to the elbow while externally rotating the arm.
result: • Positive Test: Pain on the medial aspect of the elbow or excessive gapping (laxity) compared to the uninjured side.
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment.
  • Orthobullets.com

title: Milking Maneuver
usedFor: • Detecting partial tears or insufficiency of the Posterior Band of the UCL.
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with the elbow flexed to 90° and forearm supinated (palm up).
  • Action: The examiner grasps the patient's thumb.
  • Force: Pull the thumb backward to create a valgus stress on the elbow (mimicking pulling a cow's udder).
result: • Positive Test: Reproduction of pain, apprehension, or instability at the medial elbow.
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment.
  • Orthofixar | Milking Maneuver

title: Moving Valgus Stress Test
usedFor: • Diagnosing chronic UCL tears, specifically in overhead athletes (baseball pitchers).
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands or sits with the arm abducted to 90°.
  • Action: Apply a constant valgus torque to the elbow.
  • Movement: Quickly flex and extend the elbow while maintaining the stress.
result: • Positive Test: Pain is reproduced specifically within the 'Shear Angle' (between 120° and 70° of flexion).
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment.
  • Orthofixar | Moving Valgus Stress Test

title: Elbow Lateral Pivot-Shift Test
usedFor:
• Diagnosing Posterolateral Rotatory Instability (PLRI).
  • Assessing the Lateral Ulnar Collateral Ligament (LUCL).
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with the arm overhead.
  • Action: Examiner supinates the forearm and applies a valgus force.
  • Movement: Slowly flex the elbow from full extension.
result:
• Positive Test: At ~40° of flexion, the radial head subluxates (pops out). As flexion continues, it reduces with a visible 'clunk'.
  • Note: In awake patients, apprehension is the most common sign.

title: Hook Test
usedFor: • Diagnosing complete avulsion of the Distal Biceps Tendon.
howTo:
• Position: Patient actively flexes the elbow to 90° and fully supinates the forearm (palm facing face).
  • Action: Examiner attempts to 'hook' the index finger under the lateral edge of the biceps tendon in the elbow crease.
result:
• Normal: The finger can hook approximately 1 cm under the cord-like tendon.
  • Positive (Rupture): No tendon is palpable; the finger cannot hook anything.
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment.
  • Orthofixar | Hook Test

title: Ruland Biceps Squeeze Test
usedFor: • Diagnosing Distal Biceps Tendon Rupture (similar to the Thompson test for the ankle).
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with the elbow supported in 60-80° of flexion and the forearm slightly pronated.
  • Action: Firmly squeeze the biceps muscle belly with one hand.
result: • Positive Test: The forearm does not supinate (rotate palm up) when the muscle is squeezed.
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment.
  • Orthofixar | Ruland Biceps Squeeze Test

title: The Chair Push-up Test
usedFor: • Assessing Posterolateral Rotatory Instability (PLRI) and Radial Collateral Ligament insufficiency.
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits in a chair with armrests, elbows flexed to 90°, and arms abducted.
  • Action: Patient supinates the forearms (palms holding the chair arms) and attempts to push themselves up to stand.
result: • Positive Test: Patient is apprehensive or unable to extend the elbow due to fear of dislocation (radial head subluxation).
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment.
  • Orthofixar | Chair Push-up Test

title: Cozen's Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with the elbow extended and forearm pronated (palm down).
  • Action: Patient makes a fist and actively extends/cocks the wrist backward.
  • Resistance: Examiner applies resistance against the hand movement while palpating the lateral epicondyle.
result: • Positive Test: Sudden, sharp pain over the lateral epicondyle.

title: Golfer's Elbow Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands or sits.
  • Action: Examiner palpates the medial epicondyle.
  • Movement: Passively supinate the forearm and fully extend the elbow and wrist (stretching the flexor muscles).
result: • Positive Test: Pain is reproduced at the medial epicondyle.

title: Mill's Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits comfortably.
  • Action: Examiner palpates the lateral epicondyle.
  • Movement: Passively pronate the forearm, fully flex the wrist, and extend the elbow.
result: • Positive Test: Pain over the lateral epicondyle insertion due to stretching of the Extensor Carpi Radialis Brevis.

title: Maudsley's Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with the forearm pronated.
  • Action: Examiner resists extension of the 3rd digit (middle finger) only.
result: • Positive Test: Pain at the lateral epicondyle (this specifically stresses the Extensor Carpi Radialis Brevis muscle).
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment.
  • Orthofixar | Maudsley Test

title: Chair Test
howTo:
• Task: Ask the patient to lift a chair (approx 3kg) using only three fingers (thumb, index, long).
  • Technique: The elbow must be fully extended and the forearm pronated during the lift.
result: • Positive Test: Pain at the lateral epicondyle or inability to lift the chair.

title: Reverse Cozen Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with the forearm supinated (palm up).
  • Action: Patient attempts to flex the wrist against resistance.
  • Stabilization: Examiner palpates the medial epicondyle.
result:
• Positive Test: Sharp pain at the medial epicondyle.
  • Caution: Avoid excessive force to prevent compression of the pronator musculature.

title: Elbow Range of Motion
usedFor: • Measuring functional and physiological limits of the elbow joint.
howTo:
• Flexion/Extension: Measure the angle at the elbow (Normal: 0° to 145/150°).
  • Pronation: Forearm rotation palm down (Normal: ~75°).
  • Supination: Forearm rotation palm up (Normal: ~85°).

title: Bicipital Aponeurosis Flex Test
usedFor: • Confirming complete distal biceps rupture (checking if the aponeurosis is intact).
howTo:
• Position: Patient actively flexes the wrist and makes a fist (supinated arm).
  • Action: Patient flexes the elbow to 75°.
  • Palpation: Examiner feels for the sharp edge of the bicipital aponeurosis in the medial antecubital fossa.
result: • Positive Test: The aponeurosis is not palpable (indicates rupture).

title: Ober's Test
usedFor: • Assessing tightness (contracture) of the Iliotibial Band (ITB) and Tensor Fasciae Latae (TFL).
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies on their side with the unaffected leg underneath (flexed for stability).
  • Action: Abduct the upper leg and extend the hip (to move the ITB over the greater trochanter). Keep the knee flexed at 90°.
  • Release: Stabilize the pelvis to prevent rotation and slowly lower the leg (adduction) towards the table.
result:
• Negative (Normal): The leg drops below horizontal (adducts freely).
  • Positive (Tightness): The leg remains abducted (in the air) and does not fall, indicating a tight ITB/TFL.

title: Noble Compression Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine.
  • Action: Examiner flexes the patient's knee to 90°.
  • Compression: Apply pressure to the lateral femoral epicondyle with the thumb while passively extending the knee.
result: • Positive Test: Severe pain under the thumb at approximately 30° of flexion (where the ITB rubs over the epicondyle).

title: Thomas Test
usedFor: • Assessing Hip Flexor Contracture (tightness of Iliopsoas, Rectus Femoris, or TFL).
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine at the edge of the table.
  • Action: Patient pulls one knee to the chest to flatten the lumbar spine. The other leg hangs free.
  • Observation: Observe if the straight leg rises off the table.
result:
• Positive (Tight Iliopsoas): The thigh lifts off the table.
  • Positive (Tight Rectus Femoris): The knee extends (straightens) instead of hanging at 90°.
  • Positive (Tight TFL): The leg abducts or internally rotates.

title: Staheli Test (Prone Hip Extension)
usedFor:
• Assessing Hip Flexion Contractures (specifically Iliopsoas).
  • Often used when the Thomas Test is ambiguous.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone (on stomach) with legs hanging off the table edge.
  • Action: Examiner stabilizes the pelvis with one hand and passively extends the thigh with the other.
result: • Positive Test: The pelvis rises (anterior tilt) before full hip extension is reached, indicating a fixed flexion deformity.

title: Ely's Test (Duncan-Ely)
usedFor: • Assessing spasticity or tightness of the Rectus Femoris.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone.
  • Action: Examiner passively flexes the patient's knee rapidly towards the buttock.
result: • Positive Test: The hip on the same side spontaneously flexes (buttock rises off the table) because the tight rectus femoris pulls the pelvis anteriorly.

title: Patrick's Test (FABER)
usedFor:
• Screening for hip joint pathology vs. Sacroiliac (SI) joint dysfunction.
  • FABER: Flexion, ABduction, External Rotation.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine.
  • Action: Place the foot of the affected leg on the opposite knee (Figure-4 position).
  • Force: Gently press down on the flexed knee while stabilizing the opposite hip.
result:
• Groin Pain: Indicates Hip Joint pathology (e.g., OA, Labral).
  • Posterior/Buttock Pain: Indicates SI Joint dysfunction.

title: FADDIR Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine.
  • Action: Flex the hip to 90°, then Adduct and Internally Rotate the hip.
result: • Positive Test: Reproduction of groin pain, with or without a mechanical click.

title: Log Roll Test
usedFor: • Screening for intra-articular hip pathology (synovitis, infection, fracture) without stressing muscles.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with legs extended and relaxed.
  • Action: Examiner passively rolls the thigh internally and externally (like rolling a log).
result: • Positive Test: Pain (Intra-articular pathology), Clicking (Labral tear), or excessive excursion (Ligamentous laxity).

title: Stinchfield Test
usedFor: • Differentiating between Hip Joint pathology and Lumbar/SI issues.
howTo: • Action: Patient performs an active Straight Leg Raise (SLR) to 30° against resistance.
result:
• Groin/Anterior Thigh Pain: Hip pathology.
  • Buttock/Lumbar Pain: SI joint or Lumbar pathology.

title: Hip Scour Test (Quadrant)
usedFor: • Detecting arthritis, labral tears, or capsular defects (inner and outer quadrants).
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with hip flexed to 90°.
  • Action: Apply axial compression down the femur while moving the hip in an arc of Adduction/Internal Rotation to Abduction/External Rotation.
result: • Positive Test: Pain, apprehension, or a grinding sensation (crepitus) within the joint.

title: Trendelenburg's Test
howTo:
• Action: Patient stands on one leg (the affected leg) for 30 seconds.
  • Observation: Watch the pelvis level.
result:
• Negative (Normal): The pelvis on the non-stance side rises slightly.
  • Positive: The pelvis on the non-stance (unsupported) side drops, indicating the stance leg abductors are too weak to stabilize the pelvis.

title: Craig's Test (Ryder Method)
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone with knee flexed to 90°.
  • Action: Palpate the greater trochanter while rotating the hip. Find the point where the trochanter is most prominent laterally (parallel to table).
result:
• Normal: 8-15° of internal rotation.
  • Anteversion: >15° (Associated with "toeing in").
  • Retroversion: <8° (Associated with "toeing out").
accuracy: • Highly accurate when compared to intraoperative measurements.

title: Pelvic Drop Test (Step-Down)
usedFor: • Assessing core and hip stability (similar to Trendelenburg but dynamic).
howTo: • Action: Patient stands on a step with one leg and slowly lowers the other foot to the floor.
result: • Positive Test: Pelvic drop, trunk leaning, or medial knee collapse (valgus) on the stance leg.

title: Hip Range of Motion
usedFor: • Measuring physiological limits of the hip joint.
howTo:
• Flexion: 110-120° (Knee bent).
  • Extension: 10-30°.
  • Abduction: 30-50°.
  • Adduction: 30°.
  • External Rotation: 45-60°.
  • Internal Rotation: 30-40°.

title: Ortolani's Test
usedFor: • Reducing a dislocated hip in newborns with Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH).
howTo:
• Position: Infant supine.
  • Action: Flex hips/knees to 90°. Gently Abduct the hip while lifting the greater trochanter anteriorly.
result: • Positive Test: A palpable "clunk" is felt as the femoral head slides back into the acetabulum (Reduction).

title: Barlow's Test
usedFor: • Provoking dislocation in an unstable hip (Newborn DDH).
howTo:
• Position: Infant supine.
  • Action: Adduct the hip while applying posterior pressure on the knee.
result: • Positive Test: A palpable "clunk" as the femoral head slips out of the acetabulum (Dislocation).

title: Patellar-Pubic Percussion Test
usedFor: • Detecting Occult Hip Fractures when X-rays are negative.
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine. Place stethoscope on the pubic symphysis.
  • Action: Tap (percuss) the patella of the affected leg.
result:
• Positive Test: Sound is dull or diminished on the affected side (fracture disrupts sound transmission).
  • Negative: Sound is distinct and crisp (equal to healthy side).

title: Fulcrum Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits on the edge of the bed.
  • Action: Examiner places their forearm under the patient's thigh (acting as a fulcrum) and applies downward pressure on the knee.
result: • Positive Test: Sharp pain or apprehension when the fulcrum is directly under the fracture site.

title: Pelvic Ligament Stress Tests
usedFor: • Pain provocation for Sacroiliac (SI) and pelvic ligaments (Iliolumbar, Sacrotuberous).
howTo:
• Iliolumbar: Flex hip/knee + Adduction + Axial pressure.
  • Sacroiliac: Max flexion + Adduction + Axial pressure.
  • Sacrotuberous: Max flexion + Abduction.
result: • Positive Test: Pain reproduction in the specific ligament area or dermatome.
accuracy: • Low reliability in isolation; best used in a cluster.

title: Lachman Test
usedFor:
• Assessing the integrity of the Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL), specifically the posterolateral bundle.
  • Considered the “Gold Standard” for acute ACL injury.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine. Knee flexed to 20-30°.
  • Action: Stabilize the distal femur with one hand. Grasp the proximal tibia with the other.
  • Force: Apply a quick anterior translation force to the tibia.
result: • Positive Test: Excessive anterior translation (>3mm) compared to the uninjured side, or a “soft/mushy” end-feel (lack of a sudden stop).
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment.
  • Miller, M. D. (2020). Miller's Review of Orthopaedics.

title: Anterior Drawer Test (Knee)
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with hip flexed 45° and knee flexed 90°.
  • Action: Examiner sits on the patient's foot to stabilize it. Grasp the tibia behind the calf with thumbs on the tibial tuberosity.
  • Force: Pull the tibia anteriorly.
result:
• Positive Test: >6mm of anterior displacement or a soft end-feel.
  • Note: Often false-negative in acute injuries due to hamstring spasm or hemarthrosis.

title: Pivot Shift Test
usedFor:
• Diagnosing Anterolateral Rotatory Instability (ACL + lateral capsule injury).
  • Replicates the “giving way” sensation.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with the hip slightly flexed and abducted (30°).
  • Action: Examiner holds the heel (internal rotation) and applies a Valgus force at the knee.
  • Movement: Slowly flex the knee from full extension.
result: • Positive Test: At ~20-40° of flexion, the subluxated tibia suddenly reduces (shifts back) with a palpable “clunk”.
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment.
  • Miller, M. D. (2020). Miller's Review of Orthopaedics.

title: Posterior Drawer Test (Knee)
usedFor: • Assessing the integrity of the Posterior Cruciate Ligament (PCL).
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine, hip flexed 45°, knee flexed 90°.
  • Action: Examiner sits on the foot. Place hands on proximal tibia with thumbs on tibial tuberosity.
  • Force: Push the tibia posteriorly (backward).
result: • Positive Test: Excessive posterior translation or lack of a firm end-feel.

title: Posterior Sag Sign (Godfrey's Test)
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with hips flexed 45° and knees flexed 90°.
  • Action: Observe the profile of the tibia from the side.
result: • Positive Test: The tibia “sags” or drops back visibly compared to the femur (loss of the normal tibial step-off).

title: Quadriceps Active Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, knee flexed to 90° (drawer position).
  • Action: Stabilize the foot. Ask the patient to slide their foot down the table (contracting the quadriceps).
result: • Positive Test: The tibia (which was sagging posteriorly) actively shifts forward to reduce into its normal position as the quads contract.

title: Dial Test (Tibial External Rotation)
usedFor: • Differentiating between isolated Posterolateral Corner (PLC) injury and combined PCL + PLC injury.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone (or supine).
  • Action: Externally rotate both feet at 30° of knee flexion. Repeat at 90° of knee flexion.
result:
• >10° difference at 30° only: Isolated PLC injury.
  • >10° difference at 30° AND 90°: Combined PCL + PLC injury.
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment.
  • Miller, M. D. (2020). Miller's Review of Orthopaedics.

title: Valgus Stress Test (Knee)
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine.
  • Action: Apply a Valgus (inward) force to the knee while stabilizing the ankle.
  • Test 1: Perform at 0° (full extension).
  • Test 2: Perform at 30° flexion (isolates superficial MCL).
result:
• Positive Test: Pain or excessive gapping (>5mm).
  • Note: Laxity at 0° indicates major damage to MCL + Cruciate ligaments (ACL/PCL).

title: Varus Stress Test (Knee)
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine.
  • Action: Apply a Varus (outward) force to the knee while stabilizing the ankle.
  • Test: Perform at 0° and 30° flexion.
result:
• Positive Test: Pain or excessive gapping laterally.
  • Note: Laxity at 0° suggests major Posterolateral Corner (PLC) injury.

title: Apley’s Distraction Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient prone, knee flexed 90°.
  • Action: Stabilize the thigh with your knee. Pull the tibia upwards (distraction) and rotate internally/externally.
result: • Positive Test: Pain during distraction points to Ligament injury (unloading the meniscus).

title: McMurray’s Test (Medial)
usedFor: • Diagnosing tears of the Posterior Horn of the Medial Meniscus.
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine. Flex the knee completely.
  • Action: Externally rotate the tibia and apply a Valgus stress.
  • Movement: Extend the knee while maintaining rotation/stress.
result: • Positive Test: A palpable or audible “click/thud” over the medial joint line, often with pain.

title: McMurray’s Test (Lateral)
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine. Flex the knee completely.
  • Action: Internally rotate the tibia and apply a Varus stress.
  • Movement: Extend the knee while maintaining rotation/stress.
result: • Positive Test: A palpable or audible “click/thud” over the lateral joint line.

title: Apley’s Compression Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient prone, knee flexed 90°.
  • Action: Apply a downward compression force through the heel while rotating the tibia internally and externally.
result: • Positive Test: Pain or clicking during compression (loading the meniscus).

title: Thessaly Test
usedFor: • Dynamic reproduction of meniscal loading (considered more accurate than McMurray's).
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands on the affected leg (flat foot). Examiner holds hands for balance.
  • Action: Patient flexes knee to 20°.
  • Movement: Patient rotates their body/femur internally and externally 3 times.
result: • Positive Test: Medial or lateral joint line discomfort, or a sense of locking/catching.

title: Ege's Test
howTo:
• Medial Meniscus: Patient squats with feet externally rotated (duck stance).
  • Lateral Meniscus: Patient squats with feet internally rotated (pigeon toed).
result: • Positive Test: Pain or click/snap along the joint line.

title: Bounce Home Test
usedFor: • Detecting Meniscal Tears or loose bodies blocking extension.
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine.
  • Action: Examiner flexes the knee completely, cups the heel, and allows the knee to drop passively into full extension.
result: • Positive Test: The knee does not extend fully (springy block) or causes sharp joint line pain.

title: Patellar Apprehension Test (Fairbank’s)
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, knee flexed to 30° (quads relaxed).
  • Action: Examiner slowly pushes the patella laterally.
result: • Positive Test: Patient actively contracts the quadriceps or expresses fear (apprehension) to stop the dislocation.

title: Patellofemoral Grind Test (Clarke’s Sign)
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, knee extended.
  • Action: Examiner places the web of the hand proximal to the patella and pushes it distally.
  • Command: Ask patient to contract quadriceps.
result:
• Positive Test: Retropatellar pain or inability to hold the contraction.
  • Warning: High rate of false positives; can be painful even for normal knees. Use caution.

title: Waldron’s Test
howTo: • Action: Examiner palpates the patella while the patient performs slow squats.
result: • Positive Test: Pain, crepitus (grinding), or poor patellar tracking during the movement.

title: Medial Plica Test (Mital & Hayden)
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, knee flexed to 30°.
  • Action: Push the patella medially with the thumb.
result: • Positive Test: Pain or a click is elicited medial to the patella.

title: Hughston’s Plica Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine.
  • Action: Flex knee, internally rotate tibia, push patella medially. Flex and extend the knee.
result: • Positive Test: A palpable “pop” or snap is felt under the fingers.

title: Wilson’s Test
usedFor: • Diagnosing Osteochondritis Dissecans (OCD) of the medial femoral condyle.
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with legs dangling.
  • Action: Internally rotate tibia and extend knee. Stop when pain occurs (~30°).
  • Confirmatory: Externally rotate tibia.
result: • Positive Test: Pain during internal rotation extension that is relieved by external rotation.
accuracy: • Low sensitivity; negative test does not rule out OCD.

title: Patellar Tap / Ballottement Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, knee extended.
  • Action: Milk fluid down from the thigh. Tap (push) the patella quickly against the femur.
result: • Positive Test: The patella “floats” and bounces back up (ballottable), indicating significant fluid.

title: Knee Range of Motion
howTo:
• Flexion: 0-135°.
  • Extension: 0-10° (hyperextension).
  • Rotation: Only possible when knee is flexed (Internal: 30°, External: 45°).

title: Popliteal Angle Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, hip flexed 90°.
  • Action: Extend the knee until resistance is felt.
result: • Positive Test: Angle >50° indicates abnormal tightness.

title: Garrick Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, hip/knee flexed 90°.
  • Action: Patient actively externally rotates the tibia against resistance.
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the posterolateral knee (popliteus area).

title: Neer Impingement Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated.
  • Action: Examiner stabilizes the scapula with one hand to prevent rotation.
  • Movement: Passively and forcibly elevate the patient's arm in the scapular plane with the arm internally rotated (thumb down).
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the anterior-lateral shoulder (Greater Tuberosity jamming against the acromion).

title: Hawkins-Kennedy Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated. Arm flexed to 90° and elbow flexed to 90°.
  • Action: Examiner forcibly internally rotates the shoulder.
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the supraspinatus tendon area (Supraspinatus is compressed against the coracoacromial ligament).

title: Coracoid Impingement Sign
howTo:
• Position: Arm flexed to 90°.
  • Action: Examiner adducts the arm 10-20° across the body and internally rotates it.
result: • Positive Test: Anterior shoulder pain near the coracoid process.

title: Internal Rotation Resistance Stress Test (IRRST)
howTo:
• Position: Abducted 90°, Externally Rotated 80°.
  • Action: Compare strength in External Rotation vs Internal Rotation.
result:
• Internal Impingement: Weakness in Internal Rotation.
  • Outlet Impingement: Weakness in External Rotation.

title: Empty Can Test (Jobe's)
howTo:
• Position: Arm abducted 90° in the scapular plane (scaption), internally rotated (thumb down).
  • Action: Patient resists downward pressure applied by the examiner.
result: • Positive Test: Pain or weakness compared to the other side.

title: Lift-Off Test (Gerber's)
howTo:
• Position: Hand placed behind the back (lumbar spine).
  • Action: Patient attempts to lift the hand off the back against resistance.
result: • Positive Test: Inability to lift the hand or weakness.

title: Hornblower's Sign
howTo:
• Position: Arm abducted 90°, elbow flexed 90°.
  • Action: Patient attempts to externally rotate against resistance.
result: • Positive Test: Inability to externally rotate; the arm falls into internal rotation.

title: Rent Test (Transdeltoid Palpation)
howTo:
• Position: Arm extended.
  • Action: Palpate anterior to the acromion while rotating the arm internally and externally.
result: • Positive Test: A palpable depression (sulcus) or soft spot on the greater tuberosity indicating a tear.

title: Speed's Test
howTo:
• Position: Arm flexed 90°, elbow extended, forearm supinated.
  • Action: Patient resists downward pressure.
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the bicipital groove.

title: Yergason's Test
usedFor: • Diagnosing Transverse Humeral Ligament integrity and Biceps Tendinopathy.
howTo:
• Position: Elbow flexed 90°, forearm pronated.
  • Action: Patient supinates and externally rotates against resistance.
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the bicipital groove or tendon subluxation (popping).

title: Active Compression Test (O'Brien's)
howTo:
• Part 1: Flex shoulder 90°, adduct 10°, Internally Rotate (Thumb down). Apply downward force.
  • Part 2: Externally Rotate (Palm up). Apply downward force.
result:
• Positive for SLAP: Deep joint pain in Part 1 that is relieved in Part 2.
  • Positive for AC Joint: Superficial pain on top of the shoulder in Part 1.

title: Crank Test
howTo:
• Position: Arm abducted 160° in the scapular plane.
  • Action: Apply axial load (pushing arm into socket) while internally and externally rotating the arm.
result: • Positive Test: Pain, clicking, or catching.

title: Kim Test
howTo:
• Position: Seated, arm abducted 90°.
  • Action: Apply axial load while elevating the arm diagonally upward (45°) and applying a posterior-inferior force.
result: • Positive Test: Sudden onset of posterior shoulder pain.

title: Load and Shift Test
howTo:
• Position: Seated.
  • Action: Stabilize scapula. 'Load' the humerus into the glenoid, then 'Shift' it anteriorly and posteriorly.
result: • Positive: Excessive translation (>25% or >50% of head diameter).

title: Apprehension and Relocation Test
howTo:
• Apprehension: Supine, Abduct 90°, Externally Rotate. Look for fear/guarding.
  • Relocation: Apply posterior force to humeral head. Look for relief.
result: • Positive Test: Apprehension reduced by relocation force.

title: Sulcus Sign
howTo:
• Action: Pull the arm downward (traction) at the elbow.
  • Observation: Look for a depression (sulcus) below the acromion.
result: • Positive Test: A visible sulcus >1cm.

title: Jerk Test (Posterior)
howTo:
• Position: Seated. Arm flexed 90°, Internally Rotated.
  • Action: Apply axial load (push back) while horizontally adducting the arm.
result: • Positive Test: A sudden 'clunk' or jerk as the humeral head subluxes posteriorly.

title: Shoulder Anterior Drawer Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine.
  • Action: Stabilize the scapula with one hand. With the other hand, grasp the proximal humerus and pull it anteriorly (upwards).
result: • Positive Test: Increased anterior translation compared to the unaffected side, often with a click or apprehension.

title: Cross-Body Adduction Test
howTo:
• Position: Arm flexed 90°.
  • Action: Adduct the arm horizontally across the chest.
result: • Positive Test: Pain specifically at the AC joint.

title: Wright's Test (Hyperabduction)
howTo:
• Position: Arm abducted and externally rotated. Head turned away.
  • Action: Palpate radial pulse.
result: • Positive Test: Loss of radial pulse.

title: Roos Test (EAST)
howTo:
• Position: Arms abducted 90°, elbows 90° ('Stick 'em up').
  • Action: Open and close hands for 3 minutes.
result: • Positive Test: Inability to continue, ischemic pain, or heaviness.

title: Spurling's Test
usedFor: • Diagnosing Cervical Radiculopathy (compression of a nerve root in the neck).
howTo:
• Position: Patient is seated comfortably.
  • Action: Ask the patient to extend their neck (look up) and side-bend the head towards the painful side.
  • Compression: Carefully apply a vertical downward pressure (approx. 7kg) on the top of the patient's head.
result:
• Positive Test: Reproduction of radiating pain or tingling traveling down the arm.
  • Why: This position narrows the neural foramen (the tunnel the nerve exits through). If the nerve is already compressed, the added pressure reproduces the symptoms.

title: Distraction Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine (on their back).
  • Action: Place one hand under the chin and the other under the base of the skull (occiput).
  • Force: Gently pull the head away from the body (traction force).
result:
• Positive Test: The patient's arm pain or numbness disappears or decreases.
  • Why: Traction opens the neural foramen, taking pressure off the compressed nerve root.

title: Lasegue Test (Straight Leg Raise)
usedFor: • Diagnosing Sciatica or Lumbar Disc Herniation (specifically L4-S1 nerve roots).
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with legs straight.
  • Action: Examiner slowly lifts the patient's straight leg by the heel until pain is felt.
result:
• Positive Test: Shooting pain down the leg (past the knee) between 30° and 70° of elevation.
  • Why: At this angle, the sciatic nerve is fully stretched. Pain beyond 70° is typically due to tight hamstrings or SI joint issues, not the nerve.

title: Crossed Over Lasegue (Well Leg Raise)
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine.
  • Action: Perform a Straight Leg Raise on the unaffected (pain-free) leg.
result:
• Positive Test: Pain shoots down the affected (bad) leg even though it is not moving.
  • Why: Pulling the nerve roots on the good side drags the dural sac, which indirectly pulls on the nerve roots of the bad side. This is highly specific for a herniated disc.

title: Slump Test
usedFor: • Detecting Neural Tension anywhere in the spinal tract (more sensitive than the Straight Leg Raise).
howTo:
• Step 1: Patient sits at the edge of the bed and slumps shoulders/back forward.
  • Step 2: Patient flexes neck (chin to chest).
  • Step 3: Examiner passively extends (straightens) the patient's knee.
  • Step 4: Examiner passively dorsiflexes the foot.
result:
• Positive Test: Reproduction of sciatic or neural pain.
  • Confirmation: If the patient looks up (extends neck) and the leg pain disappears, the test is positive (confirms neural origin).

title: Gaenslen Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine close to the edge of the table.
  • Action: Patient hugs one knee firmly to their chest. The other leg hangs off the edge of the table (hyperextension).
  • Force: Examiner pushes the hanging leg downward to stress the pelvis.
result: • Positive Test: Pain felt specifically in the SI joint of the hanging leg.

title: Elvey Test (Upper Limb Tension Test)
usedFor: • Screening for Cervical Radiculopathy or Peripheral Nerve Entrapment in the arm.
howTo:
• Technique: A sequence of movements to stretch specific arm nerves (similar to a Straight Leg Raise for the arm).
  • Median Nerve Bias (ULTT1): Shoulder depression + Abduction (110°) + Wrist/Finger Extension + Supination + Elbow Extension.
result: • Positive Test: Reproduction of arm symptoms that change when the patient tilts their head (sensitizing maneuver).

title: Shoulder Press Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated.
  • Action: Examiner pushes the shoulder downward while tilting the patient's head to the opposite side.
result:
• Nerve Pain: Pain/tingling on the stretched side (neck/shoulder) indicates nerve root irritation.
  • Joint Pain: Pain on the compressed side (the side the head is tilting towards) suggests facet joint pathology.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal System 3rd Ed. Book.

title: Shoulder Abduction Test (Bakody)
usedFor: • Diagnosing Cervical Radiculopathy (specifically C4, C5, or C6 nerve roots).
howTo: • Action: Patient actively lifts the painful arm and places the palm on top of their head.
result:
• Positive Test: The patient reports significant relief of arm pain.
  • Why: This position shortens the pathway of the nerve, reducing tension on the irritated nerve root.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal System 3rd Ed. Book

title: Lhermitte Sign
usedFor: • Screening for Cervical Myelopathy (spinal cord compression), Multiple Sclerosis, or severe Stenosis.
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits on the table with legs extended (long sitting).
  • Action: Examiner passively flexes the patient's neck forward (chin to chest).
result: • Positive Test: A sudden "electric shock" sensation shooting down the spine and into the arms or legs.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal System 3rd Ed. Book

title: Prone Knee Flexion Test
usedFor: • Differentiating between Lumbar, SI Joint, and Femoral Nerve pathology.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone (on stomach).
  • Action: Examiner passively flexes the patient's knee, bringing the heel toward the buttock.
result:
• Anterior Thigh Pain: Suggests Femoral Nerve tension (L2-L3).
  • Low Back Pain: Suggests Lumbar Disc lesion (L3-L4).
  • Buttock/Sacral Pain: Suggests SI Joint lesion.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal 3rd Ed. Book

title: Spinous Process Tap Test
usedFor: • Screening for serious spinal pathology like Fracture, Infection, or Disc Herniation.
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated, spine slightly flexed forward.
  • Action: Gently tap each spinous process with a reflex hammer.
result:
• Sharp Local Pain: Suggests Fracture or Infection (Osteomyelitis).
  • Radiating Pain: Suggests Disc Herniation at that level.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal 3rd Ed. Book

title: Lasegue Drop Test
usedFor: • Identifying Lumbar or SI pain caused by sudden muscle guarding.
howTo:
• Action: Perform a Straight Leg Raise until the patient feels discomfort.
  • The Drop: Suddenly let go of the leg (unexpectedly).
result:
• Positive Test: Sudden sharp pain.
  • Why: The Psoas muscle reflexively contracts to catch the falling leg, putting sudden stress on the lumbar vertebrae and SI joint.
accuracy: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal 3rd Ed. Book

title: Springing Test
usedFor: • Assessing Segmental Mobility (Stiffness vs Instability) and Pain provocation.
howTo:
• Position: Patient prone.
  • Action: Use the heel of the hand (or ulnar border) to apply gentle springing pressure (Posterior-to-Anterior) on specific vertebrae.
result:
• Hyper-mobility: The vertebra moves too much (Instability).
  • Hypo-mobility: The vertebra feels stiff/blocked.
  • Pain: Indicates local facet joint or disc irritation.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal 3rd Ed. Book

title: Bragard Test
howTo:
• Step 1: Perform a Straight Leg Raise until pain is felt.
  • Step 2: Lower the leg slightly until pain stops.
  • Step 3: Forcefully dorsiflex the foot.
result:
• Positive Test: Pain returns immediately with foot dorsiflexion.
  • Why: Dorsiflexion pulls the sciatic nerve without stretching the hamstring muscles further.
references:
• Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal 3rd Ed. Book
  • NCBI

title: Three-Phase Hyperextension Test
usedFor: • Differentiating the source of pain: Hip vs. SI Joint vs. Lumbar Spine.
howTo:
• Phase 1 (Hip): Extend the patient's hip while holding the pelvis down.
  • Phase 2 (SI Joint): Extend the hip while holding the sacrum down.
  • Phase 3 (Lumbar): Extend the hip while holding the lumbar spine down.
result: • The phase where pain appears helps localize the lesion to that specific anatomical structure.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal 3rd Ed. Book

title: Standing Flexion Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands. Examiner places thumbs on both PSIS (back dimples).
  • Action: Patient bends forward to touch their toes.
result:
• Positive Test: One thumb moves much higher (more superiorly) than the other.
  • Meaning: The side that moves higher is the blocked/stiff joint because it is stuck to the ilium and moves with it.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal 3rd Ed. Book

title: Derbolowsky Test
usedFor: • Diagnosing Functional Leg Length Discrepancy caused by pelvic rotation.
howTo:
• Step 1: Patient lies supine. Check ankle levels.
  • Step 2: Patient sits up (Long Sitting). Check ankle levels again.
result:
• Positive Test: One leg appears to change length (e.g., goes from Short to Long).
  • Meaning: Indicates the pelvis is rotated (Anterior or Posterior Innominate rotation) rather than a true short bone.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal 3rd Ed. Book

title: Yeoman Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient prone with knee flexed 90°.
  • Action: Examiner lifts the patient's extended leg (hyperextension) while stabilizing the pelvis.
result:
• Positive Test: Pain felt deep in the SI Joint.
  • Note: Pain in the front of the thigh suggests Femoral Nerve tension instead.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal 3rd Ed. Book

title: Spine Range of Motion
howTo:
• Cervical: Flexion 45°, Extension 45°, Rotation 80°, Side bending 45°.
  • Lumbar: Flexion 60°, Extension 25°, Side bending 25°.
result: • Document any restriction or pain during movement.
extra:
LEVELFLEXION/EXTENSIONLATERAL BENDINGROTATION

C1-C2101045
C2-C710-158-1010
