L-spine15-202-53-6

title: Schober Sign
howTo:
• Marking: Mark a point 5cm below and 10cm above the S2 spinous process (total 15cm).
  • Action: Patient bends forward to touch toes.
  • Measurement: Measure the distance between the two points again.
result:
• Normal: The distance should increase from 10cm to at least 15cm (>5cm expansion).
  • Positive: An increase of less than 5cm indicates restricted lumbar mobility.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal System 3rd Edition.

title: Soto-Hall Test
usedFor: • Differentiating between Bone/Ligament pathology and Muscle pathology in the neck.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine.
  • Action: Examiner stabilizes the patient's sternum with one hand and passively flexes the patient's neck (chin to chest) with the other.
result:
• Bone/Ligament Issue: Pain occurs during this passive flexion.
  • Muscular Issue: Pain occurs during active flexion (when patient lifts their own head) but diminishes during passive flexion.
references: • Clinical Tests for the Musculoskeletal System 3rd Edition.

title: Thigh Thrust Test
usedFor:
• Provocation test for Sacroiliac Joint Dysfunction.
  • Also called Posterior Shear Test.
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, hip flexed 90° and slightly adducted.
  • Action: Examiner places one hand under the sacrum and pushes the femur vertically downward with the other hand.
result: • Positive Test: Deep pain in the gluteal/SI area.
references: • Netter’s Orthopaedic Clinical Examination An Evidence-Based Approach 3rd Edition Book.

title: Beevor Sign
usedFor: • Diagnosing weakness of T10-T12 Nerve Roots or Spinal Cord Lesion.
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine with knees bent.
  • Action: Ask patient to lift their head and shoulders (partial sit-up).
  • Observation: Watch the belly button (umbilicus).
result:
• Normal: Belly button stays in the center.
  • Positive: Belly button moves upward, downward, or sideways.
  • Interpretation: Movement indicates the muscles on the opposite side are weak (e.g., if it moves up, the lower abdominals are weak).

title: Femoral Nerve Stretch Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone (on stomach).
  • Action: Examiner passively flexes the knee, bringing the heel toward the buttock. Can also extend the hip for more stretch.
result:
• Positive Test: Shooting pain into the anterior (front) thigh.
  • Note: Tightness in the quadriceps is normal; look for neural reproduction of pain.

title: Alar Ligament Test
usedFor: • Assessing Upper Cervical Instability (specifically the Alar Ligament which stabilizes C2).
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine.
  • Action: Grip the C2 spinous process (back of the upper neck) with a pincer grip.
  • Movement: Side-bend or rotate the patient's head slightly.
result:
• Normal: The C2 spinous process should move immediately in the opposite direction of the side-bend.
  • Positive (Instability): A lag or delay in C2 movement suggests ligament laxity.
references:
1. Orthofixar | Alar Ligament Test
  2. Dutton’s Orthopaedic Examination, Evaluation, And Intervention 3rd Edition.

title: CMC Grind Test
howTo:
• Position: Examiner grips the patient's thumb metacarpal bone.
  • Action: Apply an axial compressive force while rotating the thumb (grinding motion) against the trapezium.
result:
• Positive Test: Sudden sharp pain at the CMC joint, often accompanied by crepitus (grinding sound/feeling).
  • Why: This grinds the worn cartilage surfaces together, reproducing arthritis pain.

title: Lunotriquetral Ballottement Test
howTo:
• Position: Examiner stabilizes the lunate with the thumb and index finger of one hand.
  • Action: Grasp the triquetrum/pisiform with the other hand and shear it anteriorly and posteriorly against the lunate.
result:
• Positive Test: Pain, laxity, or crepitus compared to the unaffected side.
  • Why: The shearing force stresses the ligament directly.

title: Watson Test (Scaphoid Shift)
howTo:
• Position: Examiner places a thumb on the scaphoid tubercle (palmar side) and moves the patient's wrist from Ulnar Deviation into Radial Deviation.
  • Action: Apply constant dorsal pressure on the scaphoid to prevent it from flexing.
result: • Positive Test: A palpable "clunk" or shift as the scaphoid subluxes over the dorsal rim of the radius, often with dorsal pain.

title: Ulnocarpal Abutment Test
howTo: • Action: Examiner ulnarly deviates the patient's wrist while applying an axial compressive load.
result:
• Positive Test: Reproduction of ulnar-sided wrist pain, clicking, or popping.
  • Why: This compresses the TFCC and ulnar carpus against the distal ulna.
references: • Nakamura, R. et al. (1997). Journal of Hand Surgery.

title: Gamekeeper's Test (UCL Stress)
usedFor: • Diagnosing Ulnar Collateral Ligament (UCL) tears of the thumb (Skier's Thumb).
howTo:
• Action: Apply a valgus stress (pushing thumb outward) to the MCP joint.
  • Positions: Test in both full extension and 30° of flexion.
result: • Positive Test: Laxity >30° or lack of a firm end-feel compared to the healthy side.

title: Lichtman Test
howTo: • Action: Examiner stabilizes the forearm and moves the wrist from Radial Deviation to Ulnar Deviation while applying an axial load.
result: • Positive Test: A palpable "clunk" or jump as the proximal row shifts from flexion to extension.

title: Tinel's Sign (Wrist)
howTo: • Action: Examiner lightly taps (percusses) over the median nerve at the carpal tunnel (wrist crease).
result:
• Positive Test: Tingling or "electric shock" sensation in the thumb, index, and middle fingers.
  • Why: An irritated nerve is hypersensitive to mechanical percussion.

title: Phalen's Test
howTo: • Action: Patient flexes both wrists maximally by pushing the backs of hands together. Hold for 60 seconds.
result:
• Positive Test: Numbness or tingling in the median nerve distribution within 60 seconds.
  • Why: Flexion increases pressure inside the carpal tunnel, compressing the nerve.

title: Froment's Sign
howTo: • Action: Patient holds a piece of paper between the thumb and index finger (key pinch). Examiner tries to pull it away.
result:
• Positive Test: Patient flexes the thumb IP joint to hold the paper (compensating with Flexor Pollicis Longus/Median Nerve).
  • Why: Weakness of the Adductor Pollicis forces the patient to use the flexor instead.

title: Wartenberg's Sign
howTo: • Action: Patient places hand flat on table and attempts to adduct (bring together) all fingers.
result:
• Positive Test: The little finger drifts into abduction (away from the ring finger).
  • Why: Weakness of the palmar interossei muscles (Ulnar nerve) allows the extensors to pull the finger out.

title: Jeanne's Sign
howTo: • Action: Perform a key pinch (similar to Froment's Sign).
result:
• Positive Test: Hyperextension of the Thumb MCP joint.
  • Why: Loss of stabilizing force from the Adductor Pollicis.

title: Piano Key Sign
howTo:
• Position: Hand pronated.
  • Action: Examiner pushes down on the distal ulna (ulnar head) like pressing a piano key.
result: • Positive Test: The ulna springs back up (ballottable) with pain or excessive movement.

title: Finkelstein's Test
howTo: • Action: Patient makes a fist with the thumb tucked inside the fingers. Examiner passively ulnar deviates the wrist.
result:
• Positive Test: Sharp pain along the radial styloid (1st extensor compartment).
  • Why: This stretches the inflamed Extensor Pollicis Brevis and Abductor Pollicis Longus tendons.

title: Bunnel-Littler Test
usedFor: • Differentiating Intrinsic Muscle Tightness from Joint Capsule Tightness.
howTo:
• Step 1: Extend MCP joint and flex PIP joint.
  • Step 2: Flex MCP joint and flex PIP joint.
result:
• Intrinsic Tightness: PIP flexes more when MCP is flexed (relaxes the intrinsics).
  • Capsular Tightness: PIP movement is restricted equally in both positions.

title: Durkan's Test (Carpal Compression)
howTo: • Action: Examiner applies direct pressure with thumbs over the carpal tunnel for 30 seconds.
result:
• Positive Test: Onset of paresthesia (tingling) in the median nerve distribution.
  • Note: Often considered more sensitive than Phalen's or Tinel's.

title: Elson Test
howTo:
• Position: Finger flexed 90° over table edge.
  • Action: Patient tries to extend the PIP joint against resistance.
result:
• Positive Test: Weak PIP extension, but the DIP joint goes rigid/extends (lateral bands pull tight).
  • Normal: Strong PIP extension, DIP remains loose.

title: Allen Test (Vascular)
howTo:
• Step 1: Patient makes a tight fist. Examiner occludes both radial and ulnar arteries.
  • Step 2: Patient opens hand (palm is pale).
  • Step 3: Release one artery and watch for color return. Repeat for the other artery.
result:
• Normal: Hand flushes red within 3-5 seconds.
  • Positive: Persistent pallor (>5s) indicates arterial occlusion.

title: Wrist Range of Motion
howTo:
• Flexion: 65-80°.
  • Extension: 55-70°.
  • Radial Deviation: 15-20°.
  • Ulnar Deviation: 30-45°.

title: Hand Range of Motion
howTo:
• MCP Flexion: 90-100°.
  • PIP Flexion: 100-110°.
  • DIP Flexion: 70-80°.
  • Thumb Opposition: Ability to touch base of small finger.

title: Capillary Refill Test
howTo: • Action: Squeeze the fingernail until it turns white (blanches). Hold for 5 seconds and release.
result:
• Normal: Color returns in < 3 seconds.
  • Positive: Delayed return (>3s) suggests arterial insufficiency or shock.

title: Wrist & Hand General Examination
historyTaking:
Symptoms

  • Pain: Location (Radial/Ulnar/Dorsal/Volar), relation to movement or grip.
  • Swelling: Localized (Ganglion) vs. Diffuse (RA, Infection).
  • Stiffness: Morning stiffness (Inflammatory) vs. Post-activity (OA).
  • Sensory: Numbness/Tingling (Median - CTS, Ulnar - Cubital/Guyon).

Functional Loss

  • Hand dominance.
  • Difficulty with grip, pinch, buttons, or writing.
inspection:
Dorsal Aspect

  • Deformities: Mallet finger, Boutonniere, Swan neck (RA), Heberden's/Bouchard's nodes (OA).
  • Wrist: Ganglion cysts, Dinner fork deformity (Colles fracture).
  • Skin/Nails: Psoriatic pits, Clubbing, Splinter hemorrhages.

Volar (Palmar) Aspect

  • Thenar/Hypothenar Eminence: Wasting (Median/Ulnar nerve palsy).
  • Palmar Fascia: Thickening or nodules (Dupuytren's Contracture).
  • Cascading: Normal flexion cascade of fingers at rest.
palpation:
Bony Landmarks

  • Radial Styloid & Anatomical Snuffbox: Scaphoid tenderness.
  • Ulnar Styloid & TFCC: Ulnar-sided pain.
  • Carpals: Lunate, Capitate, Pisiform, Hook of Hamate.

Soft Tissue

  • Tendons: Trigger finger (nodules on A1 pulley), De Quervain's (1st dorsal compartment).
  • Pulses: Radial and Ulnar arteries (Allen test).
  • Temperature: Warmth indicates infection or inflammation.
moving:
Active & Passive ROM

  • Wrist: Flexion, Extension, Radial Deviation, Ulnar Deviation.
  • Fingers: MCP, PIP, DIP flexion/extension.
  • Thumb: Flexion, Extension, Abduction, Adduction, Opposition (Kapandji score).
  • Grip & Pinch Strength: Power grip vs. Precision pinch.

title: Elbow General Examination
historyTaking:
Pain Characteristics

  • Lateral Pain: Tennis elbow (Lateral Epicondylitis), Radial Tunnel Syndrome.
  • Medial Pain: Golfer's elbow (Medial Epicondylitis), Ulnar nerve pathology.
  • Posterior Pain: Olecranon bursitis, Triceps tendonitis.

Associated Symptoms

  • Locking (Loose bodies).
  • Numbness in ring/little finger (Cubital tunnel).
  • Trauma history (FOOSH).
inspection:
Carrying Angle

  • Normal: 5-15° valgus.
  • Cubitus Valgus: Increased angle (>15°).
  • Cubitus Varus: Gunstock deformity (Supracondylar fracture sequelae).

Swelling & Bony Contours

  • Olecranon Bursa: 'Goose egg' swelling.
  • Triangle Sign: Relationship between Epicondyles and Olecranon (Isosceles triangle in flexion, Straight line in extension).
palpation:
Bony Landmarks

  • Medial & Lateral Epicondyles (Tenderness).
  • Olecranon Process & Fossa.
  • Radial Head (Palpate during supination/pronation).

Soft Tissue & Nerves

  • Ulnar Nerve: Palpate in the cubital tunnel (funny bone) for subluxation or thickening.
  • Common Extensor Origin: Lateral epicondylitis.
  • Common Flexor Origin: Medial epicondylitis.
moving:
Range of Motion

  • Flexion/Extension: 0° to 145° (Functional arc 30-130°).
  • Supination/Pronation: Tested with elbows tucked at sides (Normal: 85°/75°).

title: Shoulder General Examination
historyTaking:
Pain Profile

  • Night Pain: Rotator cuff tear or Adhesive capsulitis.
  • Overhead Pain: Impingement syndrome.
  • Instability: History of dislocation, 'Dead arm' sensation.

Functional Limits

  • Difficulty dressing (Internal rotation), combing hair (Abduction/ER), or reaching overhead.
inspection:
Anterior View

  • Clavicle/AC Joint: Step deformity (AC separation).
  • Deltoid: Atrophy (Axillary nerve injury) or flattening (Dislocation).
  • Biceps: Popeye deformity (Long head rupture).

Posterior View

  • Scapula: Winging (Serratus anterior weakness), Sprengel's deformity.
  • Muscle Wasting: Supraspinatus/Infraspinatus fossae.
palpation:
Landmarks

  • SC Joint, Clavicle, AC Joint: Tenderness or deformity.
  • Greater Tuberosity: Rotator cuff insertion tenderness.
  • Bicipital Groove: Biceps tendon tenderness (10° internal rotation).
moving:
Active & Passive ROM

  • Forward Flexion: 0-180°.
  • Abduction: 0-180° (Note Painful Arc 60-120°).
  • External Rotation: At side and at 90° abduction.
  • Internal Rotation: Hand behind back (measure vertebral level reached).
  • Scapulohumeral Rhythm: Observe ratio of glenohumeral to scapulothoracic movement (2:1).

title: Foot & Ankle General Examination
historyTaking:
Mechanism of Injury

  • Inversion (ATFL sprain) vs. Eversion (Deltoid/Syndesmosis).
  • "Pop" felt/heard (Achilles rupture, Fracture).

Pain & Symptoms

  • Start up pain: Plantar fasciitis.
  • Walking on uneven ground: Subtalar instability/arthritis.
  • Shoe wear patterns: Uneven wear indicates biomechanical faults.
inspection:
Weight Bearing (Standing)

  • Arch: Pes Planus (Flat foot) or Pes Cavus (High arch).
  • Hindfoot: Valgus (Heel turns out) or Varus (Heel turns in).
  • Toes: Hallux Valgus (Bunion), Claw toes, Hammer toes.
  • Too Many Toes Sign: Viewed from behind (Tibialis Posterior dysfunction).

Non-Weight Bearing

  • Calluses, Corns, Plantar warts.
  • Swelling or bruising (Ecchymosis).
palpation:
Bony Tenderness

  • Ottawa Rules: Posterior edge of Lateral/Medial Malleolus, Base of 5th Metatarsal, Navicular.

Soft Tissue

  • Achilles Tendon: Thickening, gaps, or tenderness.
  • Plantar Fascia: Medial calcaneal tubercle tenderness.
  • Ligaments: ATFL, CFL, Deltoid.
  • Pulses: Dorsalis Pedis, Posterior Tibial.
moving:
Ankle Joint

  • Dorsiflexion (20°) & Plantarflexion (50°).

Subtalar Joint

  • Inversion & Eversion (Check for stiffness).

Forefoot

  • Adduction/Abduction.
  • Toe flexion/extension.

title: Knee General Examination
historyTaking:
Pain & Mechanical Symptoms

  • Locking: Meniscal tear or loose body.
  • Giving Way: ACL injury or Patellar instability.
  • Swelling: Immediate (ACL/Fracture - Hemarthrosis) vs. Delayed (Meniscus/Synovitis).
  • Anterior Pain: Patellofemoral syndrome (Stairs, sitting prolonged).
inspection:
Standing

  • Alignment: Genu Varum (Bow-legged) vs. Genu Valgum (Knock-kneed).
  • Popliteal Fossa: Baker's Cyst.

Supine

  • Effusion: Loss of parapatellar dimples.
  • Muscle: Vastus Medialis (VMO) wasting (compare girth 10cm above patella).
  • Patella: Position (Alta/Baja), Squinting patella.
palpation:
Joint Line

  • Palpate medial and lateral joint lines in 90° flexion (Meniscal tenderness).

Patellofemoral

  • Retropatellar tenderness, Apprehension test.

Effusion Tests

  • Patellar Tap: For large effusions.
  • Wipe/Stroke Test: For minimal fluid.
moving:
Range of Motion

  • Flexion (0-140°) and Extension (0°).
  • Check for Hyperextension (Genu Recurvatum).
  • Note any Extensor Lag (Inability to actively extend despite full passive ROM).

title: Hip General Examination
historyTaking:
Pain Location

  • Groin (C-Sign): True hip joint pathology (OA, Labral tear).
  • Lateral/Trochanteric: Trochanteric Bursitis.
  • Buttock: SI Joint or Lumbar Spine referral.

Functional History

  • Limp: Painful (Antalgic) vs. Painless (Trendelenburg/Short leg).
  • Difficulty putting on shoes/socks (loss of flexion/rotation).
inspection:
Gait

  • Trendelenburg Gait: Dropping of pelvis on unaffected side (Abductor weakness).
  • Antalgic Gait: Shortened stance phase on painful side.

Stance

  • Pelvic Tilt: ASIS/PSIS levels (Leg length discrepancy).
  • Lumbar Lordosis: Excessive lordosis may mask fixed flexion deformity.
palpation:
Landmarks

  • Anterior: ASIS, Femoral Pulse (Femoral Triangle).
  • Lateral: Greater Trochanter (Bursitis tenderness).
  • Posterior: PSIS, Ischial Tuberosity.
moving:
Special ROM Tests

  • Thomas Test: Reveals Fixed Flexion Deformity.
  • Flexion/Extension: 120°/30°.
  • Abduction/Adduction: 45°/30°.
  • Internal/External Rotation: Tested in flexion (90°) and extension. Loss of Internal Rotation is an early sign of OA.

title: Navicular Drop Test
usedFor: • Quantifying the amount of foot pronation (flattening) during weight-bearing.
howTo:
• Step 1: Mark the navicular tuberosity (bony bump on the inner foot).
  • Step 2: Measure its height from the floor while the patient sits (non-weight bearing).
  • Step 3: Measure the height again while the patient stands (weight bearing).
result: • Positive Test: A difference (drop) of more than 10mm indicates excessive pronation.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Coleman Block Test
usedFor: • Differentiating between flexible and rigid hindfoot varus deformities.
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands with the heel and outer foot on a wooden block (2.5-4cm thick).
  • Action: Allow the first metatarsal (big toe side) to drop off the edge of the block to touch the ground.
result:
• Flexible: The heel alignment corrects to neutral when the big toe drops.
  • Rigid: The heel remains in varus (tilted inward) despite the change in position.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Tinel's Sign (Ankle)
usedFor: • Testing for Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome (Posterior Tibial Nerve compression).
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits or lies comfortably.
  • Action: Lightly tap (percuss) over the nerve located behind the medial malleolus (inner ankle bone).
result: • Positive Test: Tingling or "pins and needles" sensation radiates into the foot or toes.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Cotton Test
usedFor: • Assessing instability of the syndesmosis (lateral talar shift).
howTo:
• Stabilization: Hold the lower leg steady with one hand.
  • Action: Grasp the foot (talus) and apply a sideways (lateral) force.
result: • Positive Test: Feeling a "clunk" or excessive movement (>3-5mm) indicates widening of the syndesmosis.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Ottawa Ankle Rules
usedFor: • Determining the need for X-rays after acute ankle injury.
howTo:
• Palpation: Press on the posterior edge (last 6cm) of the lateral and medial malleoli.
  • Palpation: Press on the base of the 5th metatarsal and the navicular bone.
  • Function: Ask the patient to walk 4 steps (weight bear) immediately after injury and in the clinic.
result: • Positive: Pain in specific bony areas OR inability to bear weight indicates a need for X-rays to rule out fracture.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Too Many Toes Sign
usedFor:
• Assessing for hyperpronation (flatfoot) and tibial torsion.
  • Identifying dysfunction of the posterior tibial tendon.
howTo:
• Position: Have the patient stand comfortably while facing away from you.
  • Observation: Look at the patient's feet from directly behind.
  • Count: Observe how many toes are visible on the outer (lateral) side of the heel.
result:
• Normal: Only the fifth toe and part of the fourth toe are visible.
  • Positive: Seeing more than two toes (e.g., 3 or 4) indicates the heel is in valgus and the forefoot is abducted (flattened).
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.
  • Miller, M. D. (2020). Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (8th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Single Leg Heel Raise
usedFor:
• Testing the strength and function of the Posterior Tibial Tendon.
  • Diagnosing acquired flatfoot deformity.
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands on one foot (using a wall or chair for balance if needed).
  • Action: Ask the patient to rise up onto the ball of the foot (tip-toe) repeatedly.
  • Observation: Watch the movement of the heel.
result:
• Normal: The patient can lift the heel easily, and the heel tilts inward (inverts) as it rises.
  • Positive: The patient cannot lift the heel, experiences pain, or the heel remains straight/tilted outward (valgus).
references: • Miller, M. D. (2020). Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (8th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Silfverskiöld Test
usedFor: • Differentiating between tightness in the Gastrocnemius muscle versus the Soleus muscle.
howTo:
• Step 1: With the patient's knee extended (straight), passively dorsiflex the foot and assess the range.
  • Step 2: Flex the patient's knee to 90 degrees and passively dorsiflex the foot again.
result:
• Tight Gastrocnemius: Dorsiflexion is limited when the knee is straight but improves significantly when the knee is bent.
  • Tight Soleus/Achilles: Dorsiflexion is limited in both positions (knee straight and knee bent).
references: • Miller, M. D. (2020). Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (8th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Crossed-Leg Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits in a chair.
  • Action: Have the patient cross the injured leg over the uninjured knee (like a 'Figure 4' position), so the mid-calf rests on the knee.
  • Pressure: Gently push down on the medial side of the injured knee.
result: • Positive Test: Pain is reproduced at the distal ankle syndesmosis (between the tibia and fibula).
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Dorsiflexion Maneuver
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits or lies supine.
  • Action: The examiner passively moves the ankle into maximal dorsiflexion and eversion.
  • Duration: Hold this position for 5 to 10 seconds.
result: • Positive Test: Patient reports numbness, tingling, or pain radiating into the foot (toes or sole).
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Figure-Eight Measurement
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with the ankle in a neutral position.
  • Action: Wrap a tape measure around the ankle in a specific order:
    1. Start at the Tibialis Anterior tendon.
    2. Go to the distal 5th metatarsal base.
    3. Cross over the Navicular tuberosity.
    4. Pass below the lateral malleolus.
    5. Pass below the lateral medial malleolus and back to the start.
result: • Positive: A measurable difference (e.g., > 1-2 cm) compared to the uninjured side indicates significant swelling.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Mulder's Sign
usedFor: • Diagnosing Morton's Neuroma (interdigital neuroma), typically between the 3rd and 4th toes.
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits or lies supine.
  • Action: Squeeze the metatarsal heads together with one hand (transverse arch compression).
  • Palpation: With the other hand, apply pressure to the interspace between the toes and push upwards from the sole.
result: • Positive Test: A distinct "click" is felt or heard, often accompanied by pain that reproduces the patient's symptoms.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Royal London Hospital Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone (on stomach) with feet hanging off the table.
  • Step 1: Palpate the Achilles tendon for a tender lump while the foot is relaxed (slightly plantar flexed).
  • Step 2: Ask the patient to actively dorsiflex the foot (pull toes up) to stretch the tendon, and palpate the tender spot again.
result: • Positive Test: The tenderness significantly decreases or disappears when the tendon is stretched (dorsiflexed). If pain remains constant, it may indicate paratendonitis rather than tendinopathy.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Arc Sign
usedFor: • Differentiating between Achilles Tendinosis (tendon degeneration) and Paratendonitis (inflammation of the sheath).
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone.
  • Action: Identify an area of localized swelling on the Achilles tendon.
  • Movement: Ask the patient to actively dorsiflex and plantarflex the ankle.
result:
• Tendinosis (Positive Arc): The swelling moves up and down with the tendon movement.
  • Paratendonitis: The swelling/thickening remains stationary while the tendon glides inside it.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Buerger’s Test
usedFor: • Assessing arterial blood supply to the leg (Peripheral Arterial Disease).
howTo:
• Step 1 (Elevation): Patient lies supine. Elevate the leg to 45° for 3 minutes. Observe foot color.
  • Step 2 (Dependency): Patient sits up quickly with legs hanging over the edge. Observe the refill time and color return.
result:
• Positive Test:
    • Elevation: The foot turns pale (pallor) quickly.
    • Dependency: The foot turns bright red (rubor) which is known as "reactive hyperemia."
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Peroneal Tendon Dislocation Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone with the knee bent to 90°.
  • Action: Ask the patient to actively pull the foot up and out (Dorsiflexion + Eversion).
  • Resistance: Apply resistance to this movement while observing the area behind the lateral malleolus (outer ankle bone).
result: • Positive Test: The tendons snap or pop out from behind the malleolus, often accompanied by pain or apprehension.
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.
  • Miller, M. D. (2020). Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (8th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Paper Grip Test
usedFor:
• Testing the strength of the intrinsic muscles of the foot (toe plantar flexion).
  • Screening for intrinsic muscle weakness (e.g., in diabetic neuropathy or claw toe deformity).
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with the foot flat on the floor.
  • Action: Place a piece of paper (or business card) under the patient's toes.
  • Command: Ask the patient to curl their toes to grip the paper while you try to pull it away.
result: • Positive Test: Inability to hold the paper (weakness) compared to the uninjured side.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Triple Compression Stress Test
usedFor:
• Diagnosing Tarsal Tunnel Syndrome (Posterior Tibial Nerve entrapment).
  • Considered more sensitive than Tinel's Sign for this condition.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine or sits with the leg relaxed.
  • Action: The examiner places the ankle in full Plantar Flexion and Inversion (Supination).
  • Compression: Simultaneously, the examiner applies constant digital pressure over the posterior tibial nerve (behind the medial malleolus). Maintain for 30 seconds.
result: • Positive Test: Reproduction of numbness, tingling, or paresthesia in the foot or toes (plantar distribution).
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Calcaneal Squeeze Test
usedFor:
• Diagnosing Calcaneal Stress Fractures.
  • Differentiating heel pain sources (e.g., Plantar Fasciitis vs. Stress Fracture).
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits or lies prone with feet hanging off the table.
  • Action: The examiner cups the heel (calcaneus) with both hands.
  • Compression: Apply firm, steady pressure from both sides (medial and lateral) to compress the calcaneus.
result:
• Positive Test: Significant pain is produced by the compression.
  • Note: Plantar fasciitis is typically tender on the plantar aspect, whereas stress fractures are tender with medial-lateral compression.
references:
• Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.
  • Miller, M. D. (2020). Miller's Review of Orthopaedics (8th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Fibular Translation Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies in a side-lying position.
  • Stabilization: The examiner stabilizes the tibia with one hand.
  • Action: Grasp the distal fibula (lateral malleolus) with the other hand and attempt to translate it anteriorly and posteriorly relative to the tibia.
result: • Positive Test: Pain or excessive movement compared to the uninjured side.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Anterior Impingement Test (Molloy-Bendall)
usedFor:
• Diagnosing Anterior Ankle Impingement (Footballer's Ankle).
  • Identifying soft tissue or bony entrapment at the anterior joint line.
howTo:
• Position: Patient is seated.
  • Action: The examiner firmly palpates the anterolateral ankle joint line with the thumb.
  • Movement: While maintaining pressure, quickly and passively move the ankle from plantar flexion into full forced dorsiflexion.
result: • Positive Test: Sharp, localized pain at the site of compression, often accompanied by a feeling of blocking.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Functional Hallux Limitus Test
usedFor: • Determining if the big toe (Hallux) lacks extension specifically during weight-bearing (functional limitation).
howTo:
• Step 1 (Non-Weight-Bearing): Passively dorsiflex the big toe while the patient is sitting. Note the range (usually 60-90°).
  • Step 2 (Weight-Bearing): Passively dorsiflex the big toe while the patient stands normally.
  • Step 3 (Loaded): Passively dorsiflex the toe while pushing up on the first metatarsal head (simulating gait load).
result: • Positive Test: Normal range of motion exists in non-weight-bearing, but movement is significantly restricted (stiff) during weight-bearing or loading.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Shin Palpation Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits or lies supine.
  • Action: Palpate the distal two-thirds of the posteromedial border of the tibia (shin bone).
  • Pressure: Apply firm pressure along the bone edge.
result:
• Positive Test: Pain that is widespread (diffuse) along the medial border (covering at least 5 cm).
  • Note: Focal (pinpoint) tenderness suggests a stress fracture instead of MTSS.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Prone Anterior Drawer Test
usedFor:
• Assessing the Anterior Talofibular Ligament (ATFL).
  • Often considered more sensitive than the supine test because gravity helps relax the calf muscles.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies on their stomach (prone) with feet hanging off the end of the table.
  • Stabilization: Press down on the back of the calf (lower leg) with one hand to stabilize it against the table.
  • Action: Cup the heel with the other hand and push it downward (anteriorly) towards the floor.
result:
• Positive Test: Excessive anterior movement (dropping) of the foot compared to the uninjured side.
  • Note: A "dimple" (suction sign) may appear on the outer ankle.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Dorsiflexion Compression Test
usedFor:
• Diagnosing Syndesmosis Injury (High Ankle Sprain).
  • Differentiating syndesmosis pain from other ankle pain.
howTo:
• Step 1: Patient stands and lunges forward into maximum ankle dorsiflexion. Note if this causes pain.
  • Step 2: Examiner uses both hands to firmly compress the medial and lateral malleoli (ankle bones) together.
  • Step 3: While maintaining this compression, the patient lunges into dorsiflexion again.
result: • Positive Test: The pain felt in Step 1 is significantly decreased or eliminated during Step 2 (compression stabilizes the separation, reducing pain).
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Medial Subtalar Glide Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine or sits with the leg stabilized.
  • Action: Examiner grasps the talus with one hand to hold it steady.
  • Movement: With the other hand, grasp the calcaneus (heel) and glide it medially (inwards) relative to the talus.
result: • Positive Test: Excessive movement or opening (gapping) compared to the normal side.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Tuning Fork Test
howTo:
• Action: Strike a tuning fork (128 Hz or 256 Hz) to make it vibrate.
  • Placement: Place the base of the vibrating fork directly onto the bone at the site of suspected fracture.
result:
• Positive Test: Sharp, sudden increase in pain at the fracture site.
  • Note: If pain is absent, a stress fracture is less likely, but X-ray/MRI may still be needed.
accuracy: • Sensitivity: Variable; good for ruling out fractures when negative.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Subtalar Neutral
usedFor: • Determining the neutral position of the rearfoot for biomechanical assessment and orthotic casting.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone with feet hanging off the table.
  • Action: Grasp the 4th and 5th metatarsal heads with one hand and gently dorsiflex the foot until resistance is felt.
  • Palpation: With the other hand, palpate the head of the talus (front of the ankle) on both the medial and lateral sides.
  • Movement: Slowly swing the foot side-to-side (supination/pronation) until the talus head feels equal on both sides (not protruding more on one side).
result: • Observation: This position is "Subtalar Neutral." The angle of the heel relative to the leg is then measured to identify varus or valgus deformities.
references: • Magee, D. J. (2021). Orthopedic Physical Assessment (7th ed.). Elsevier.

title: Elbow Varus Stress Test
usedFor: • Assessing the integrity of the Lateral Collateral Ligament (LCL) complex.
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with elbow flexed to 20-30° to unlock the olecranon.
  • Action: Stabilize the humerus with one hand and apply a varus (outward) force to the forearm with the other.
result: • Positive Test: Pain on the lateral aspect or excessive gapping (laxity) compared to the uninjured side.

title: Tabletop Relocation Test
usedFor:
• Diagnosing Posterolateral Rotatory Instability (PLRI).
  • Useful for patients who are too apprehensive for the Pivot-Shift test.
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands facing a table.
  • Action: Patient places the hand of the symptomatic arm on the edge of the table (forearm supinated) and pushes up.
  • Relocation: The examiner applies pressure to the radial head to prevent subluxation, usually relieving pain.
result: • Positive Test: Symptoms (pain/apprehension) occur when pushing up but are relieved when the examiner stabilizes the radial head.

title: Posterolateral Rotatory Drawer Test
usedFor: • Testing for PLRI (Rotatory instability of the radius/ulna unit).
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with the arm overhead and elbow flexed 90°.
  • Action: Examiner stabilizes the humerus and applies a posterior/rotatory force to the radius and ulna, attempting to subluxate them off the capitellum.
result: • Positive Test: A visible skin dimple (suction sign) or apprehension/clunk indicating subluxation.

title: Prone Push-Up Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone (on stomach) with elbows flexed 90° and forearms supinated.
  • Action: Patient attempts to push up off the floor.
result: • Positive Test: The radial head subluxates (dislocates) causing apprehension or the arm to give way.

title: Resisted Supination Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with elbow flexed 90° and forearm pronated.
  • Action: Patient actively supinates against examiner resistance.
result:
• Tennis Elbow: No pain or mild pain.
  • Radial Tunnel (Positive): Significant pain deep in the extensor muscle mass (distal to the epicondyle).

title: Reverse Mill's Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits.
  • Action: Examiner passively extends the wrist and fully extends the elbow (stretching the flexors).
result: • Positive Test: Pain over the medial epicondyle.

title: Biceps Crease Interval (BCI)
howTo: • Action: Measure the distance between the antecubital crease (elbow fold) and the cusp of the biceps muscle belly.
result: • Positive Test: A distance > 6.0 cm (or significantly greater than the unaffected side) indicates a rupture/retraction.

title: Passive Pronation Test
usedFor: • Identifying a partial tear or thickening of the bicipital aponeurosis/tendon.
howTo: • Action: Passively pronate the patient's forearm to the end range.
result: • Positive Test: Loss of pronation range compared to the other side suggests bicipital pathology (the tendon winds around the radius).

title: Triceps Squeeze Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone with the arm hanging over the table edge.
  • Action: Squeeze the triceps muscle belly.
result: • Positive Test: Absence of elbow extension (arm does not straighten slightly) indicates rupture.

title: Tinel's Sign (Elbow)
howTo: • Action: Lightly tap (percuss) the ulnar nerve in the groove behind the medial epicondyle.
result: • Positive Test: Tingling/paresthesia in the ulnar distribution (4th and 5th fingers).

title: Elbow Flexion Test
howTo: • Action: Patient fully flexes the elbow with wrist extended and holds for 3 to 5 minutes.
result: • Positive Test: Tingling or numbness in the ulnar distribution within 5 seconds to 5 minutes.

title: Pressure Provocation Test
howTo: • Action: Examiner applies direct digital pressure over the ulnar nerve at the cubital tunnel for 60 seconds with the elbow flexed.
result: • Positive Test: Reproduction of symptoms (numbness/tingling) in the 4th/5th digits.

title: Scratch Collapse Test
howTo:
• Step 1: Patient resists bilateral shoulder external rotation (baseline strength).
  • Step 2: Examiner scratches the skin over the suspected nerve entrapment site.
  • Step 3: Immediately re-test shoulder strength.
result: • Positive Test: Temporary loss of muscle resistance (collapse) due to cutaneous gating reflex.

title: Elbow Extension Test
howTo: • Action: Ask the patient to fully extend the elbow.
result:
• Negative (Normal): If patient can fully extend, a fracture is highly unlikely (Rule out fracture).
  • Positive: Inability to fully extend suggests fracture or significant effusion.

title: Valgus Extension Overload Test
howTo: • Action: Rapidly extend the patient's elbow while applying a valgus stress.
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the posteromedial elbow (olecranon tip abutting the fossa) or a mechanical lock/grind.

title: Carrying Angle Measurement
howTo: • Action: Patient stands in anatomical position. Measure the angle between the humerus and ulna.
result:
• Normal: Men: 5-10°; Women: 10-15°.
  • Abnormal: >15° (Cubitus Valgus) or <5° (Cubitus Varus/Gunstock Deformity).

title: Modified Milking Maneuver
usedFor: • Detecting Ulnar Collateral Ligament (UCL) insufficiency (specifically the posterior bundle).
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with the arm adducted, elbow flexed to 70°, and the thumb pointing backward (shoulder external rotation).
  • Action: Examiner pulls the thumb outward/backward to apply valgus stress.
result: • Positive Test: Pain, apprehension, or medial joint opening. This position isolates the UCL more specifically than the standard maneuver.

title: Posterolateral Apprehension Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with the arm overhead.
  • Action: Examiner grasps the patient's wrist and elbow, applying a force that mimics the Pivot-Shift (supination + valgus + flexion).
result: • Positive Test: The patient verbally expresses fear ('apprehension') that the elbow will dislocate, or resists the movement, even if no actual clunk occurs.

title: Polk's Test
usedFor: • Differentiating Lateral Epicondylitis from other lateral elbow pain.
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits in a chair.
  • Step 1: Patient lifts a weight (approx 2.5 kg) with the forearm pronated (palm down).
  • Step 2: Patient lifts the same weight with the forearm supinated (palm up).
result: • Positive Test: Pain is provoked during the pronated lift but is absent or significantly less during the supinated lift.

title: Triceps Reflex Test
usedFor: • Assessing the integrity of the C7 Nerve Root and Triceps Tendon.
howTo:
• Position: Arm abducted and elbow relaxed (hanging).
  • Action: Examiner strikes the triceps tendon just proximal to the olecranon with a reflex hammer.
result:
• Normal: Visible extension of the elbow.
  • Abnormal: Diminished (hyporeflexia) or absent reflex indicates C7 pathology or tendon rupture.

title: Biceps Reflex Test
usedFor: • Assessing the integrity of the C5-C6 Nerve Roots and Biceps Tendon.
howTo:
• Position: Patient sits with arm relaxed on their lap.
  • Action: Examiner places their thumb on the biceps tendon in the cubital fossa and strikes their own thumb with the reflex hammer.
result:
• Normal: Visible flexion of the elbow.
  • Abnormal: Diminished reflex suggests C5/C6 impingement or tendon pathology.

title: Galeazzi Sign (Allis Sign)
usedFor: • Screening for Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH) or Leg Length Discrepancy.
howTo:
• Position: Child lies supine with hips flexed to 45° and knees flexed to 90°, feet flat on the table.
  • Action: Observe the height of the knees from the front.
result:
• Positive Test: One knee is lower than the other.
  • Interpretation: If the knee is lower, it suggests femoral shortening or hip dislocation on that side.

title: Telescoping Sign (Piston Test)
usedFor: • Detecting a dislocated hip in a child (DDH).
howTo:
• Position: Child lies supine with the hip and knee flexed to 90°.
  • Action: Examiner grasps the thigh and pushes down (posteriorly) and pulls up (anteriorly).
result: • Positive Test: Excessive movement (pistoning) of the thigh relative to the pelvis, indicating the femoral head is not seated in the acetabulum.

title: 90-90 Straight Leg Raise
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with both hips and knees flexed to 90°. Patient grasps behind their own knees.
  • Action: Patient actively extends one knee as much as possible while keeping the hip at 90°.
result: • Positive Test: Inability to extend the knee to within 20° of full extension (i.e., knee angle is <160°).

title: Thigh Thrust Test (Posterior Shear)
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine. Examiner flexes the patient's hip to 90° with the knee bent.
  • Action: Examiner applies a forceful vertical thrust down the femur (axial load) to shear the ilium posteriorly.
result: • Positive Test: Reproduction of familiar pain in the SI joint.
accuracy: • Sensitivity: 88% (High reliability when combined in a cluster).

title: Gaenslen's Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine close to the edge of the table.
  • Action: Patient holds one knee to the chest (flexion). The other leg hangs off the edge of the table (hyperextension).
  • Force: Examiner applies overpressure to both legs.
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the SI joint.
accuracy: • Part of the Laslett Cluster for SIJ pain.

title: McCarthy Sign
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with both hips flexed.
  • Action: Examiner extends the affected hip, first in external rotation, then repeats in internal rotation.
result: • Positive Test: Reproduction of groin pain or a mechanical 'catch'/'click'.

title: Leg Length Measurement (True vs Apparent)
usedFor: • Differentiating True (Structural) from Apparent (Functional) Leg Length Discrepancy (LLD).
howTo:
• True LLD: Measure from ASIS to Medial Malleolus (fixed bony points).
  • Apparent LLD: Measure from Umbilicus (belly button) to Medial Malleolus (affected by pelvic tilt).
result: • Positive: A difference of >1-1.5 cm is considered clinically significant.

title: Heel Strike Test (Anvil Test)
usedFor: • Screening for Hip Fracture or severe joint pathology.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with legs straight.
  • Action: Examiner firmly strikes the bottom of the patient's heel with a fist.
result: • Positive Test: Sharp pain in the groin or hip joint (force travels up the femur).

title: Rectus Femoris Contracture Test
usedFor: • Differentiating Rectus Femoris tightness from other hip flexors.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with one leg hanging over the table edge (knee flexed 90°).
  • Action: Patient pulls the other knee to the chest. Examiner attempts to flex the hanging knee further.
result: • Positive Test: Resistance to knee flexion or the hip flexes more as the knee is bent, indicating rectus tightness.

title: Abduction Test (Hart's Sign)
usedFor: • Screening for Developmental Dysplasia of the Hip (DDH) in older infants where Ortolani is no longer effective.
howTo:
• Position: Infant lies supine with hips flexed 90°.
  • Action: Examiner gently abducts both hips simultaneously.
result: • Positive Test: Limitation of abduction on the affected side (often <60°).

title: Weber-Barstow Maneuver
usedFor: • Normalizing pelvic alignment before measuring Leg Length Discrepancy (LLD).
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with hips and knees flexed.
  • Action: Patient lifts the pelvis off the table (bridge) and then relaxes back down. Examiner passively extends the legs.
  • Result: This resets the pelvis. The examiner then compares the level of the medial malleoli to assess gross leg length difference.
result: • Positive Test: Visible difference in the level of the malleoli indicates LLD.

title: Supine-to-Sit Test (Long Sitting Test)
usedFor: • Differentiating Functional Leg Length Discrepancy caused by Sacroiliac Joint (SIJ) rotation.
howTo:
• Step 1: Patient lies supine. Examiner compares malleoli levels.
  • Step 2: Patient sits up (Long Sitting). Examiner compares malleoli levels again.
result:
• Positive Test: One leg appears longer in supine but shorter in sitting (or vice versa).
  • Interpretation: This suggests an Anterior or Posterior Innominate Rotation (SIJ dysfunction) rather than a true short bone.

title: Gillet's Test (Marching Test)
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands.
  • Palpation: Examiner places one thumb on the PSIS (Posterior Superior Iliac Spine) and the other on the Sacrum (S2).
  • Action: Patient flexes the hip (lifts knee) on the side being palpated.
result:
• Normal: The PSIS drops (moves inferiorly) relative to the sacrum.
  • Positive (Hypomobile): The PSIS moves up or remains stationary, indicating a 'blocked' or stuck SI joint.

title: Yeoman's Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone.
  • Action: Examiner extends the patient's hip while keeping the knee flexed to 90° (this rotates the ilium anteriorly).
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the SI joint area.

title: Flamingo Test
usedFor: • Provocation test for Pubic Symphysis or SI Joint lesions.
howTo: • Action: Patient stands on one leg (like a flamingo) and hops once or maintains balance.
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the pubic symphysis or SI joint on the stance side.

title: Posterior Labral Tear Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine.
  • Action: Examiner moves the hip from full Flexion, Adduction, and Internal Rotation → into Extension, Abduction, and External Rotation.
result: • Positive Test: Groin pain, buttock pain, or a palpable apprehension/click.

title: Ischiofemoral Impingement Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies on the unaffected side.
  • Action: Examiner passively extends, adducts, and externally rotates the affected hip.
result: • Positive Test: Pain deep in the buttock lateral to the ischium.

title: Slocum Test (Rotatory Drawer)
usedFor:
• Diagnosing Anteromedial (AMRI) or Anterolateral (ALRI) Rotatory Instability.
  • This is a modification of the Anterior Drawer Test.
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, knee flexed to 90°.
  • AMRI Test: Rotate the foot externally 15° and pull the tibia anteriorly.
  • ALRI Test: Rotate the foot internally 30° and pull the tibia anteriorly.
result: • Positive Test: Excessive anterior translation on the side of rotation compared to the unaffected knee.
accuracy: • Sensitivity: Variable; often limited by hamstring guarding.

title: Jerk Test of Hughston
usedFor:
• Diagnosing Anterolateral Rotatory Instability (ALRI) (ACL + Lateral Capsule).
  • Similar to the Pivot Shift but performed in reverse (Flexion to Extension).
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, hip flexed 45°, knee flexed 90°.
  • Action: Apply a Valgus stress and Internal Rotation to the tibia.
  • Movement: Slowly extend the knee.
result: • Positive Test: At ~20-30° of flexion, the tibia (which was subluxated anteriorly) suddenly reduces with a palpable 'jerk' or 'clunk'.

title: Losee Test
usedFor:
• Diagnosing Anterolateral Rotatory Instability (ALRI).
  • Considered a dynamic variation of the Pivot Shift.
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine.
  • Action: Apply a Valgus stress and push forward on the fibular head (anterior force) while extending the knee from flexion.
result: • Positive Test: A palpable 'clunk' is felt near full extension as the lateral tibial plateau subluxes anteriorly.

title: Noyes Flexion-Rotation Drawer Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine. Examiner holds the leg in 20-30° flexion.
  • Action: The tibia is held in neutral and then allowed to drop posteriorly (if gravity allows) or manually reduced from a subluxated position by flexing the knee.
result: • Positive Test: The tibia reduces (moves backward) and the femur rotates externally as the knee is flexed, indicating ACL insufficiency.

title: External Rotation Recurvatum Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, legs extended.
  • Action: Examiner lifts both legs by the great toes.
  • Observation: Look at the tibial tuberosity and knee hyperextension.
result: • Positive Test: The affected knee falls into excessive hyperextension (recurvatum) and the tibia rotates externally (tibial tuberosity shifts laterally).

title: Reverse Pivot Shift Test (Jakob)
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, knee flexed to 90°.
  • Action: Externally rotate the tibia and apply a Valgus stress.
  • Movement: Slowly extend the knee.
result: • Positive Test: At ~20-30° of flexion, the posteriorly subluxated tibia reduces (shifts forward) with a clunk.

title: Loomer’s Posterolateral Rotary Instability Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, hips and knees flexed to 90°.
  • Action: Examiner grasps the heels and maximally externally rotates both tibias.
result: • Positive Test: Excessive external rotation on the injured side, often accompanied by the tibia dropping posteriorly (sag).

title: Crossover Test (Arnold)
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands and crosses the unaffected leg over the injured leg.
  • Action: Patient rotates the torso away from the injured side (creating tibial internal rotation) or towards it.
result: • Positive Test: Pain or a sensation of the knee giving way implies rotational instability.

title: Childress Sign (Duck Walk Test)
howTo: • Action: Patient squats fully and attempts to walk forward (waddle) in the squat position.
result: • Positive Test: Pain, inability to perform the walk, or a clicking sound in the posterior knee.

title: Brush / Stroke Test (Swipe Test)
usedFor: • Detecting Minimal Knee Effusion (swelling) that Ballottement might miss.
howTo:
• Step 1: Stroke upwards on the medial side of the knee (to empty the fluid).
  • Step 2: Stroke downwards on the lateral side of the knee.
result: • Positive Test: A wave of fluid creates a bulge on the medial side. Graded from Trace to 3+.
accuracy: • More sensitive than Ballottement for small amounts of fluid (4-8ml).

title: Fluctuation Test
howTo:
• Action: Examiner places one hand on the suprapatellar pouch and the other on the anterior knee joint.
  • Technique: Press down with one hand and feel for fluid displacement (fluctuation) under the other hand.
result: • Positive Test: Fluid is felt moving back and forth between the hands.

title: Indentation Test
howTo:
• Action: Patient lies supine. Examiner presses a thumb into the soft tissue next to the patella.
  • Observation: Watch how quickly the indentation disappears.
result:
• Effusion: Indentation disappears instantly (fluid refalls).
  • Synovial Thickening: Indentation remains or disappears slowly (pitting edema/thickening).

title: McConnell Test
howTo:
• Step 1: Patient performs isometric quad contractions at varying angles (0, 30, 60, 90, 120°). Note pain.
  • Step 2: If pain occurs, examiner pushes the patella medially (glide) and repeats the contraction.
result: • Positive Test: Pain is significantly reduced when the medial glide is applied.

title: Q-Angle Measurement
howTo:
• Points: Draw a line from ASIS to mid-patella, and from tibial tuberosity to mid-patella.
  • Measurement: Measure the angle at the intersection.
result:
• Normal: Men: 13°; Women: 18°.
  • Abnormal: >18° increases risk of subluxation and PFPS.

title: Tubercle Sulcus Angle
howTo:
• Position: Knee flexed to 90°.
  • Observation: Observe the position of the tibial tuberosity relative to the center of the patella.
result:
• Normal: Tuberosity is vertically aligned with the center of the patella.
  • Abnormal: Tuberosity is positioned laterally (>10°), indicating lateral tracking.

title: Plica Stutter Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated with knees at 90°.
  • Action: Patient slowly extends the knee while the examiner places a finger over the patella.
result: • Positive Test: The patella 'stutters' or jumps irregularly between 40-60° of flexion (instead of gliding smoothly).

title: Lever Sign (Lelli's Test)
usedFor:
• Diagnosing Anterior Cruciate Ligament (ACL) rupture.
  • Effective even in patients with large legs or acute guarding.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with legs straight.
  • Setup: Examiner places a fist under the proximal calf (acting as a fulcrum).
  • Action: Examiner pushes down firmly on the distal quadriceps (thigh).
result:
• Negative (Normal): The heel lifts off the table (the ACL pulls the tibia up).
  • Positive (Rupture): The heel remains on the table (the tibia slides anteriorly instead of lifting the foot).

title: Prone Lachman Test
usedFor:
• Diagnosing ACL Rupture.
  • Useful for examiners with small hands or patients with large legs.
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies prone. The examiner places a hand under the distal thigh (just above the knee) to support it.
  • Action: Examiner pushes the tibia anteriorly (downward towards the floor) while stabilizing the femur.
result: • Positive Test: Excessive anterior displacement or soft end-feel.

title: Adson's Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated. Examiner palpates the radial pulse.
  • Action: Patient rotates their head towards the tested side and extends the neck while taking a deep breath and holding it.
  • Extension: Examiner laterally rotates and extends the patient's shoulder.
result: • Positive Test: Disappearance or significant diminution of the radial pulse.

title: Allen Test (Shoulder)
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated. Arm abducted 90°, elbow flexed 90° (like Roos test).
  • Action: Patient rotates head away from the tested side. Examiner palpates the radial pulse.
result: • Positive Test: Diminished or absent radial pulse.

title: Costoclavicular Syndrome Test (Military Brace)
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands in an exaggerated military posture (shoulders drawn back and down).
  • Action: Examiner palpates the radial pulse. Patient extends the neck.
result: • Positive Test: Absence of radial pulse.

title: Halstead Maneuver
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated. Examiner palpates radial pulse.
  • Action: Examiner pulls the arm downward (traction) while the patient hyperextends the neck and rotates the head to the opposite side.
result: • Positive Test: Absence or diminution of pulse.

title: AC Shear Test (Paxinos Sign)
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated.
  • Action: Examiner cups hands over the shoulder: one hand on the clavicle, the other on the spine of the scapula.
  • Force: Squeeze the hands together (shearing the AC joint).
result: • Positive Test: Pain localized at the AC joint.

title: Anterior Slide Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands with hands on hips, thumbs pointing backward.
  • Action: Examiner stabilizes the scapula and applies an anterior-superior force at the elbow (pushing the humerus up and forward).
result: • Positive Test: Pain, click, or pop in the anterior shoulder.

title: Clunk Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine.
  • Action: Abduct arm fully overhead. Examiner places a fist behind the humeral head to push it anteriorly while rotating the humerus.
result: • Positive Test: A grinding sound or 'clunk' sensation.

title: Compression-Rotation Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, arm abducted 90°, elbow 90°.
  • Action: Examiner applies an axial load (compresses humerus into glenoid) while rotating the arm internally and externally (like grinding a pestle in a mortar).
result: • Positive Test: Snapping, catching, or pain.

title: Pain Provocation Test (Mimori)
howTo:
• Position: Arm abducted 90-100°.
  • Action: Patient supinates the forearm fully, then pronates fully. Examiner passively externally rotates the arm in both positions.
result: • Positive Test: Pain is worse in pronation than in supination (Biceps anchor twists in pronation).

title: Feagin Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands with arm abducted 90° resting on the examiner's shoulder.
  • Action: Examiner clasps hands over the proximal humerus and pushes it down and forward.
result: • Positive Test: Apprehension on the patient's face or a visible sulcus.

title: Anterior Release Test (Surprise Test)
howTo:
• Step 1: Perform the Relocation Test (pushing humeral head posterior).
  • Step 2: Suddenly release the posterior pressure.
result: • Positive Test: Sudden return of pain or apprehension (Surprise!).

title: Whipple Test
howTo:
• Position: Arm flexed to 90° and adducted until the hand is opposite the contralateral shoulder.
  • Action: Patient resists a downward force.
result: • Positive Test: Pain or weakness. This position compresses the articular surface of the cuff.

title: Upper Cut Test
howTo:
• Position: Elbow flexed 90°, forearm supinated.
  • Action: Patient performs a rapid 'upper cut' punch motion (bringing hand towards chin) while examiner resists.
result: • Positive Test: Pain or a painful pop in the anterior shoulder.

title: Pectoralis Major Contracture Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, hands clasped behind head.
  • Action: Patient attempts to relax elbows down to the table.
result: • Positive Test: Elbows do not reach the table surface.

title: Lateral Scapular Slide Test (Kibler)
howTo:
• Measurement: Measure distance from inferior angle of scapula to spinous process (T7) in 3 positions:
  • 1. Arms at side.
  • 2. Hands on hips.
  • 3. Arms abducted 90°.
result: • Positive Test: Asymmetry > 1.5cm between sides in any position.

title: Yokum Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient places the hand of the affected arm on the opposite shoulder.
  • Action: Patient actively lifts the elbow against examiner resistance.
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the shoulder.

title: Posterior Impingement Sign
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine. Arm abducted 90-120° and maximally externally rotated.
  • Action: Examiner passively rotates the arm further into external rotation.
result: • Positive Test: Deep posterior shoulder pain.

title: Pectoralis Minor Length Test
usedFor: • Assessing tightness of the Pectoralis Minor (causes anterior scapular tilt).
howTo:
• Position: Patient lies supine with arms at sides.
  • Observation: Examiner observes the position of the acromion process relative to the table.
result: • Positive Test: The posterior shoulder does not rest on the table (excessive anterior tilt). Distance from table to acromion > 2.6cm.

title: Latissimus Dorsi Length Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, knees bent (to flatten lumbar spine).
  • Action: Patient raises both arms overhead into full flexion.
result: • Positive Test: Arms cannot rest flat on the table overhead without the lumbar spine arching (compensatory extension).

title: Olecranon-Manubrium Percussion Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated with arms crossed.
  • Action: Examiner places a stethoscope on the manubrium (sternum) and taps the olecranon (elbow).
result: • Positive Test: Dull or diminished sound on the injured side compared to the uninjured side (loss of bone conduction).

title: ULTT 1 (Median Nerve Bias)
usedFor: • Screening for Cervical Radiculopathy or Median Nerve tension.
howTo:
• Action: Depress shoulder, Abduct arm 110°, Extend wrist/fingers, Supinate forearm, Extend elbow.
  • Sensitizing: Tilt head to opposite side.
result: • Positive Test: Reproduction of symptoms (tingling/pain) that changes with head tilt.

title: ULTT 2a (Median Nerve Bias)
howTo: • Action: Depression of shoulder, Abduction 10°, Elbow extension, External rotation, Wrist extension.
result: • Positive Test: Symptom reproduction.

title: ULTT 2b (Radial Nerve Bias)
howTo: • Action: Depression of shoulder, Abduction 10°, Elbow extension, Internal rotation, Wrist flexion (Waiters Tip position).
result: • Positive Test: Symptom reproduction in radial distribution.

title: ULTT 3 (Ulnar Nerve Bias)
howTo: • Action: Depression of shoulder, Abduction 90°, External rotation, Elbow Flexion, Wrist Extension (Mask position).
result: • Positive Test: Symptom reproduction in ulnar distribution (4th/5th fingers).

title: Vertebral Artery Test (VBI)
usedFor: • Screening for Vertebrobasilar Insufficiency (VBI) before performing cervical manipulation.
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine with head off the table.
  • Action: Examiner passively extends the neck, laterally bends, and rotates the head to the same side. Hold for 30 seconds.
  • Observation: Patient keeps eyes open. Talk to them to check speech.
result:
• Positive Test: Dizziness, nystagmus (eye flickering), slurred speech (dysarthria), or nausea (The '5 Ds').
  • Why: This position compresses the vertebral artery on the contralateral side. If the ipsilateral artery is blocked/stenotic, blood flow to the brainstem drops.

title: Sharp-Purser Test
usedFor:
• Assessing Atlantoaxial Instability (Transverse Ligament rupture).
  • Commonly used in Rheumatoid Arthritis or Down Syndrome.
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated, neck slightly flexed.
  • Action: Examiner places one hand on the forehead and the other stabilizing the C2 spinous process.
  • Force: Apply a posterior force on the forehead (pushing head back).
result:
• Positive Test: A clunk sensation or relief of symptoms.
  • Why: If the ligament is torn, C1 slips forward on flexion. Pushing the head back reduces (relocates) C1, relieving the spinal cord compression.

title: Jackson's Compression Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient seated.
  • Action: Patient rotates head to the painful side.
  • Force: Examiner applies downward axial compression without side-bending or extension.
result: • Positive Test: Radiating pain into the arm.

title: Craniocervical Flexion Test (CCFT)
usedFor:
• Assessing endurance/control of Deep Neck Flexors (Longus Colli/Capitis).
  • Crucial for whiplash and cervicogenic headache.
howTo:
• Setup: Patient supine with biofeedback cuff under neck (inflated to 20mmHg).
  • Action: Patient performs a chin tuck (nod) to increase pressure to 22, 24, 26, 28, and 30mmHg, holding for 10s each.
result: • Positive Test: Inability to hold pressure or use of superficial muscles (SCM/Platysma).
references: • Jull, G. et al. (2008). Whiplash, Headache, and Neck Pain.

title: Costoclavicular Syndrome Test (Military Brace)
usedFor: • Diagnosing TOS caused by compression between the clavicle and first rib.
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands in a 'Military' posture (shoulders drawn back and down).
  • Action: Examiner palpates the radial pulse while the patient extends their neck.
result:
• Positive Test: Absence of radial pulse.
  • Why: Depressing the shoulders narrows the costoclavicular space, compressing the artery.

title: Chest Expansion Test
howTo:
• Position: Place tape measure around the chest at the 4th intercostal space (nipple line).
  • Action: Measure the difference between maximal exhalation and maximal inhalation.
result:
• Normal: Difference > 5cm.
  • Positive: Difference < 2.5cm indicates restriction of the rib cage/thoracic spine.

title: Kemp's Test (Quadrant Test)
howTo:
• Position: Patient standing (or seated).
  • Action: Examiner guides patient into Extension, Side-bending, and Rotation towards the painful side. Then applies overpressure.
result:
• Local Pain: Facet Joint pathology.
  • Radiating Pain: Nerve root compression (Stenosis).

title: Stork Test (One-Leg Standing Extension)
howTo:
• Position: Patient stands on one leg.
  • Action: Patient extends their spine (leans back).
result:
• Positive Test: Sharp, localized pain in the low back on the standing side.
  • Why: This position maximally loads the facet joints and pars interarticularis.

title: Segmental Instability Test
howTo:
• Step 1: Patient prone with legs off table. Examiner presses PA on lumbar spine (painful).
  • Step 2: Patient lifts legs (activating muscles). Examiner presses again.
result:
• Positive Test: Pain is present in Step 1 but disappears in Step 2.
  • Why: Muscle activation stabilizes the segment, eliminating the pain.
accuracy: • Sensitivity: High for identifying responders to stabilization exercise.

title: Hoover Test
usedFor: • Diagnosing Malingering or Functional Weakness (checking if effort is genuine).
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine.
  • Action: Examiner cups hands under both heels. Ask patient to lift one leg (Straight Leg Raise).
result:
• Normal: When lifting one leg, the patient instinctively pushes down with the opposite heel (examiner feels pressure).
  • Positive (Malingering): Patient claims they cannot lift the leg, but the examiner feels no downward pressure from the opposite heel (indicates lack of effort).

title: SI Joint Compression Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient side-lying (painful side up).
  • Action: Examiner applies a downward vertical force on the iliac crest (compressing the pelvis).
result: • Positive Test: Reproduction of SI joint pain.

title: SI Joint Distraction Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine.
  • Action: Examiner crosses arms and applies an outward/downward force on both ASIS (spreading the anterior pelvis).
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the gluteal area/SI joint.

title: Sacral Thrust (Sacral Compression)
howTo:
• Position: Patient prone.
  • Action: Examiner applies a vertical downward force directly on the center of the sacrum.
result: • Positive Test: Reproduction of pain in the SI joint.

title: Babinski Sign
howTo: • Action: Run a blunt object along the lateral border of the foot, curving across the metatarsal heads.
result:
• Normal: Toes curl downward (flexion).
  • Positive: Big toe extends (goes up) and other toes fan out.
accuracy: • Specificity: 99% (Highly indicative of CNS problem in adults).

title: Ankle Clonus Test
howTo: • Action: Quickly and forcefully dorsiflex the patient's foot and hold it there.
result: • Positive Test: Rhythmic, beating oscillations (foot pulses against hand) > 3 beats.

title: Hoffmann's Sign
usedFor: • Screening for Cervical Myelopathy or Upper Motor Neuron (UMN) lesions.
howTo: • Action: Examiner supports the patient's hand and flicks the nail of the middle finger downward.
result:
• Positive Test: Involuntary flexion (clawing) of the index finger and thumb.
  • Meaning: Indicates spinal cord compression or brain lesion.

title: Kernig's Sign
howTo:
• Position: Patient supine, hip and knee flexed to 90°.
  • Action: Examiner attempts to passively extend the knee.
result: • Positive Test: Severe stiffness/pain in the hamstrings/neck prevents extension.

title: Romberg Test
howTo:
• Step 1: Patient stands feet together, eyes open (Visual input active).
  • Step 2: Patient closes eyes (Visual input removed).
result:
• Positive Test: Significant sway or loss of balance only when eyes are closed.
  • Meaning: Indicates proprioceptive loss (sensory ataxia). If unstable with eyes open, it suggests cerebellar pathology.

title: TFCC Load Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient's wrist in ulnar deviation.
  • Action: Examiner applies an axial compressive load and rotates the forearm (grinding the carpus against the TFCC).
result: • Positive Test: Pain, clicking, or crepitus on the ulnar side of the wrist.

title: Press Test (Sit-to-Stand)
howTo: • Action: Patient sits in a chair with armrests. Ask them to push themselves up using their hands (wrists extended).
result:
• Positive Test: Significant ulnar-sided wrist pain that prevents the patient from lifting their weight.
  • Why: Weight-bearing extends the wrist and axially loads the ulnar side.
references: • Lester, B. et al. (1995). Journal of Hand Surgery.

title: Supination Lift Test
howTo:
• Position: Patient places palms on the underside of a table (forearms supinated).
  • Action: Patient attempts to lift the table.
result:
• Positive Test: Localized pain on the ulnar side of the wrist.
  • Why: Loading the wrist in supination compresses the TFCC.

title: Sweater Finger Sign
howTo: • Action: Ask the patient to make a fist.
result:
• Positive Test: The distal phalanx (fingertip) of the affected finger does not flex; it sticks out straight.
  • Why: The FDP tendon is detached and retracted, so the DIP joint cannot flex.

title: Boyes Test
howTo:
• Position: Examiner holds the patient's finger in extension at the PIP joint.
  • Action: Ask the patient to flex the DIP joint.
result:
• Positive Test: The patient cannot flex the DIP joint.
  • Why: If the central slip is intact, extending the PIP relaxes the lateral bands, allowing DIP flexion. If ruptured, the lateral bands tighten, preventing DIP flexion.

title: Linburg-Comstock Anomaly Test
usedFor: • Detecting an anatomical connection between the FPL (Thumb) and FDP (Index) tendons.
howTo:
• Position: Examiner restricts the index finger in extension.
  • Action: Patient attempts to flex the thumb IP joint.
result:
• Positive Test: Pain in the forearm or inability to flex the thumb.
  • Meaning: The tendons are adhered/connected, causing tenosynovitis symptoms.

title: Reverse Phalen's Test
howTo: • Action: Patient extends both wrists (Prayer position) for 60 seconds.
result:
• Positive Test: Numbness/tingling in the median nerve distribution.
  • Note: Increases carpal tunnel pressure more than standard Phalen's in some patients.

title: Semmes-Weinstein Monofilament Test
usedFor: • The Gold Standard for testing cutaneous pressure threshold (Protective Sensation).
howTo:
• Action: Examiner presses a monofilament (usually 2.83 or 5.07) against the fingertip until it bends.
  • Response: Patient says "Yes" when they feel the touch (eyes closed).
result:
• Green (2.83): Normal sensation.
  • Blue (3.61): Diminished light touch.
  • Purple (4.31): Diminished protective sensation.
  • Red (6.65): Loss of deep pressure sensation.

title: Two-Point Discrimination (Static)
howTo:
• Tool: Use a specialized disk or caliper.
  • Action: Touch the fingertip with one or two points randomly.
  • Limit: Determine the smallest distance the patient can distinguish as two points.
result:
• Normal: < 6mm.
  • Fair: 6-10mm.
  • Poor: 11-15mm.

title: Anatomical Snuffbox Tenderness
howTo:
• Position: Patient extends the thumb to define the snuffbox (between EPL and EPB tendons).
  • Action: Examiner palpates firmly in the floor of the snuffbox.
result:
• Positive Test: Sharp, localized pain.
  • Warning: High sensitivity; missed scaphoid fractures can lead to avascular necrosis.

title: Scaphoid Compression Test
howTo: • Action: Examiner holds the patient's thumb and applies an axial compression force along the metacarpal towards the scaphoid.
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the scaphoid region.

title: Murphy's Sign (Lunate)
howTo:
• Action: Patient makes a fist.
  • Observation: Examiner looks at the level of the 3rd metacarpal (middle knuckle).
result:
• Normal: The 3rd knuckle projects higher than the 2nd and 4th.
  • Positive: The 3rd knuckle is level with the others (it has "dropped").

title: Grip Strength (Dynamometry)
usedFor: • Objective measurement of overall hand function and weakness.
howTo:
• Tool: Jamar Dynamometer.
  • Position: Elbow flexed 90°, forearm neutral. Handle usually at position 2.
  • Action: Patient squeezes as hard as possible. Average of 3 trials.
result: • Bell Curve: Compare to normative data or contralateral side (10% difference is normal for dominance).

title: Pinch Strength Test
usedFor: • Assessing intrinsic muscle strength and nerve integrity.
howTo:
• Tool: Pinch Gauge.
  • Tip Pinch: Thumb tip to index tip (Median nerve/FPL/FDP).
  • Key Pinch: Thumb pad to lateral index (Ulnar nerve/Adductor Pollicis).
  • Palmar Pinch: Thumb pad to index/middle pads (Median nerve).
result: • Compare to norms and opposite side.

title: Finger Extension Test (Shuck Test)
howTo: • Action: Patient flexes the wrist slightly. Examiner applies resistance while patient tries to extend the fingers.
result: • Positive Test: Pain in the wrist.

title: Wrinkle Test (Shrivel Test)
usedFor:
• Assessing Peripheral Nerve Function (Sympathetic innervation).
  • Used in children or uncooperative patients.
howTo: • Action: Place the patient's hand in warm water for 5-20 minutes.
result:
• Normal: Skin wrinkles (pruning).
  • Positive: Skin remains smooth (denervated skin does not wrinkle).

title: Ulnar Fovea Sign
howTo:
• Position: Hand neutral.
  • Action: Examiner presses deeply into the interval between the ulnar styloid process and the flexor carpi ulnaris tendon (the 'soft spot').
result:
• Positive Test: Reproduction of severe pain or tenderness.
  • Note: High sensitivity (95%) for foveal disruptions.

title: Test for Extrinsic Tightness
howTo: • Action: Flex the fingers while the wrist is extended. Then flex the fingers while the wrist is flexed.
result: • Positive Test: Fingers flex easily with wrist extended, but are restricted/cannot flex fully when the wrist is flexed.

title: Test for Intrinsic Tightness
howTo: • Action: Hold MCP joint in extension and flex the PIP. Then flex the MCP and flex the PIP.
result: • Positive Test: PIP flexion is limited when MCP is extended, but improves when MCP is flexed.
